existingChapters.title: Foreword: Anything Is Possible
existingChapters.summary: Sets the book's purpose for young and older readers: that background, poverty and parental occupation are not destiny, and that a clear early decision plus a positive mindset and focused work ethic can shape a lifetime. Frames the memoir as an evidence-led argument that consistency and self-belief beat circumstance, and previews the Trust-and-Inspire philosophy and faith that thread through every chapter.

existingChapters.title: A Miner's Son: Early Home Life and Formative Years
existingChapters.summary: Expands the picture of growing up the youngest of five siblings in semi-detached mine housing on the East Rand, with a devout Christian father who spent his life underground at Sub Nigel Gold Mine and a shy, industrious mother who held the home together on a miner's wage. Captures the moral economy of the mine community, the weekly church rhythm, the brother who blazed the apprentice-Boilermaker trail, and the early decision at sixteen to follow into the mine while privately refusing to make it a life.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# A Miner's Son: Early Home Life and Formative Years

If you want to understand the working life I have written about in the rest of this book, you have to begin in a small, semi-detached house on the East Rand, in the rows of married quarters that the mine had thrown up around itself the way a tree throws up shade. Our house was not the smallest on the row and it was not the largest. It had the regulation number of rooms, the regulation patch of garden, and the regulation pair of clothes-lines that ran between two metal poles in the back yard. From the kitchen window, on a clear day, you could see the head-gear of Sub Nigel rising above the roofs of the houses opposite. From the front step, on every day of the week, you could hear the shift hooter call my father, and a thousand other fathers, to and from the cage that took them underground.

That hooter was the metronome of my childhood. We did not own a clock that was more authoritative than it. The morning hooter said the day-shift men were due on the surface. The afternoon hooter said the next shift was filing in. The Sunday morning silence — when the wheels above the shaft turned slower, and the row of houses smelled of polish instead of coal-dust — was, in its way, the loudest hooter of all. It told you which day belonged to the mine, and which day belonged to the Lord.

## The youngest of five

I was the youngest of five children, and that fact alone shaped a great deal of who I became. There is a particular vantage point that the last child in a large family is given. By the time you arrive, the household has already been running for some years without you. The older brothers and sisters have already been to school, already been to confirmation classes, already learned which floorboard creaked when you tried to come in after a curfew. You inherit, as the youngest, a set of household rules that have already been tested by the people in front of you. Some of those rules have been worn smooth. Others have been worn out.

What I inherited, more than anything, was a sense that the family was an enterprise. It had a budget. It had a schedule. It had a quiet operational discipline that was not announced from the head of the table but was simply assumed by everyone who sat at it. My father went down on his shift. My mother kept the house. My brothers and sisters did their chores. And I, as the youngest, slotted in at the bottom of an established order and learned, very early, that you did not interrupt the running of a household to draw attention to yourself.

It is a lesson I have carried into every plant I have ever managed. A factory, like a household, has a rhythm that precedes you. The new manager who walks in and tries to impose himself on that rhythm in his first week is the manager who loses the floor. The new manager who walks in quietly, watches the rhythm for a while, and only then begins to make his adjustments, is the manager the floor eventually adopts as one of its own. I learned that, not on a factory floor, but at the bottom of a five-child rotation in a mine house on the East Rand.

## A father who went underground

My father was a miner at Sub Nigel Gold Mine, and he spent the working life of a man under the surface of South Africa. He was not an educated man in the formal sense. He had been taught what he needed by the church and by the shaft and by the long, quiet evenings at the kitchen table, and he carried those three educations into every conversation he ever had with his children.

He was, by temperament, the more outgoing of my parents. Where my mother was shy almost to the point of being invisible in a room of strangers, my father had a friendly word for the men on the row and a steady handshake for the elders at our church. He believed that a man's word was the only currency that travelled with him from the surface to underground and back again. A miner who could not be trusted by the men on his shift would not be trusted by the rope, the lamp, or the rock. My father trusted those three things, and he meant to be the kind of man whom they, in their own way, could trust back.

I have written elsewhere in this book about the lung disease that eventually took him at sixty-three. I do not want to dwell on it again here. I want to say only this. Long before the cough that announced the disease, long before the doctors confirmed what the row of houses already knew, my father had given his children something that the disease was never going to be able to take away from him. He had given us a way of being a man. Quiet, devout, dependable, on time, and unwilling to make a fuss about any of those qualities. I have spent fifty-seven working years trying, with mixed success, to live up to that description. I am still trying.

There was an order in which he held the two great loyalties of his life. He believed in his shaft and in his Lord, in roughly that order on a weekday and in the reverse order on a Sunday. He saw no contradiction between the two. The Lord, in his account of the world, expected a man to go down on his shift and to do the work he had been given to do. The shaft, in turn, expected a man to keep the Sabbath holy and to bring his family to church when the wheels above the head-gear had been allowed to slow for a day. The two institutions were not in competition for his loyalty. They were, in his mind, two halves of the same instrument.

## A mother who held the line

My mother was the other half of the arrangement, and in some ways the more remarkable half. She was a shy woman by nature, and the world she lived in did not give her many opportunities to outgrow that shyness. She had married a miner. She had raised five children on a miner's wage. She had stretched a household budget around school shoes and church clothes and the occasional small treat that a child remembers for fifty years afterwards because it had cost her a sacrifice nobody told the child about.

She held the line in the way that women of her generation, on the East Rand and elsewhere, held the line. Without complaint. Without theatre. Without the modern vocabulary that we now use to describe the invisible labour of a household. The dishes were done because she did them. The shirts were ironed because she ironed them. The school lunches were packed because she packed them. The garden ran because she ran it. The household, in plain language, was a small, well-managed enterprise that posted no annual results, paid no dividend, and yet returned, over five decades, the only return that has ever truly compounded in my life.

She had one habit in particular that I want to record here, because it explains a great deal of what came afterwards. She woke me, in the dark, before the rest of the row had started its day, to study. I was a working apprentice by then. The trade test was years off, the engineering diploma further off still, and the school books and correspondence material were stacked on the corner of the kitchen table where the sugar bowl ought to have been. My mother would have a single light on in the kitchen, a kettle on the stove, and a chair pulled up to the corner where my books were waiting. She did not say much. She did not need to. The chair was pulled up. The light was on. The kettle was warm. The message was unmistakable. The day belonged to the mine. The hour before the day belonged to my studies.

I have, in my working life, written quite a lot about discipline. About showing up early. About doing the unglamorous work before the visible work begins. About the man who has already pulled on his overalls in the change-room when the second alarm goes. Every one of those habits was originally an inheritance from a shy woman with a single kitchen light. She did not call it leadership. She called it making breakfast.

She died a year after my father, at fifty-four. I have always believed that fifty-four was the age her body recorded but that her real age, by then, was much older. She had given the household everything she had. There had been nothing in reserve for the year in which her husband was no longer underground and her youngest children had begun to leave the row. I think of her, often, when I see a quiet woman in a meeting room hold an entire family of a business together without being noticed. The world does not name those women publicly. Their husbands and their sons are supposed to. I am naming her here, in print, fifty years after the fact, because I should have done it more often when she was alive.

## Five rooms, two parents, five children and a Lord

The house on the row had five rooms and a verandah. Two parents, five children and an unseen Sixth occupant whose presence was as real to my parents as the kitchen table. We prayed before we ate. We prayed before we slept. We prayed in the morning before my father went down. We did not pray ostentatiously. We did not pray for the neighbours to hear. We prayed the way you do something that has been done every day for so long that the words have become almost invisible — leaving behind only the orientation of the family, like the lines on a compass that a navigator no longer has to read because his hand already knows the direction.

The moral economy of the row was governed by a small, unwritten code. You did not steal from a man whose lamp had been on the same hook as your father's. You did not gossip about a family whose washing was on the same line as yours. You did not raise your voice in the kitchen if it could be heard in the kitchen next door. You did not take the Lord's name in vain in front of the children, and you did not, under any circumstance, miss church on the Sunday after a payday — because a man who could not put his shilling in the plate on a Sunday after he had been paid was a man who had forgotten the order of his loyalties.

There were five children and a single household budget, and the budget did not stretch to luxuries. Christmas was a real Christmas, but it was a measured one. New school uniforms came in at the start of the year and were expected to last to the end of it. Shoes were repaired before they were replaced. There was a careful, almost engineered, husbandry of small resources that my mother practised without ever calling it that. I have walked into businesses, forty years later, that did not husband their working capital with a fraction of the discipline that my mother brought to a tin of polish and a pair of school shoes.

## The brother who went in first

Of my four older siblings, the one who left the deepest mark on the trajectory of my own working life was the brother who became an apprentice Boilermaker at Sub Nigel before I did. He blazed the trail, and a younger brother, watching from the row, learned more from his going in than from any career advice the household could otherwise have offered.

There is a particular kind of older brother who, without ever sitting you down to explain anything, teaches you the shape of the next ten years of your life by living the next ten years of his own in front of you. Mine came home in overalls, and the overalls had the unmistakable smell of a mine workshop on them — that mixture of oil, hot steel, sweat and the faintly mineral dust that comes off the rock when a bench saw has been running through it all afternoon. He came home tired in a way that I had not, until then, seen a young man be tired. He came home, after his trade test, with the quiet confidence of someone who had been tested by a panel of older artisans and had been judged competent. He did not boast. Boilermakers do not boast. Their work boasts for them, in the form of a weld that holds and a bracket that runs true.

I watched him, and I made a decision long before I was old enough to articulate it. I would do what he had done. I would serve an apprenticeship as a Boilermaker. I would go in through the same door that he had gone in through, and I would take from the mine the trade test he had taken from it. But — and this is the part of the decision that I did not say out loud at the dinner table at fourteen — I would not stay. I would use the mine as a starting point, in the same way my brother was using it as a starting point, and I would, in due course, find the door that led out of it.

I owe him a debt of a particular kind. He did not know that he was being studied. Older brothers seldom do. They are too busy being themselves to notice that a younger brother is taking notes. He went in, did the work, passed the trade test, and got on with his life. The younger brother behind him absorbed every step of that journey and folded it into a plan he would not fully execute for another four years.

## A decision at sixteen

I left school at sixteen and signed on as an apprentice Boilermaker at Sub Nigel Gold Mine. The decision was not a dramatic one. It was, in the household economy of a miner's family on the East Rand, the obvious decision. The mine was the largest employer in our small world. It paid apprentices a real wage. It taught a real trade. It had taken my father and my brother before me, and it was prepared to take me on the same terms.

What was less obvious — what I did not announce to anyone at the time, not even to my mother, who would have been the only person in the house who would have entirely understood it — was the second half of the decision. I had decided, on the same morning that I had decided to go in, that the mine would be a place I started from and not a place I stayed in. I do not know how a sixteen-year-old comes to a decision of that clarity. I suspect it is a combination of things — the cough my father had begun to develop, the brother who was already a year ahead of me, the chair in the kitchen and the light on at four in the morning, the verses my mother had read to me from the side table in the front room, and the simple, unsentimental arithmetic of a household that did not have enough money for a son who chose the easier path of staying where he had started.

I went in through the door of the mine, as the boys of our row had gone in for two generations. I rode the cage down, on my first morning, with men whose lamps were already old and whose coughs were already the coughs my father would later develop. I learned the trade of a Boilermaker with the seriousness that my brother had modelled and that my father expected. I did not, on that first morning, tell anyone what I had privately decided. I simply held, against the inside of my chest, the quiet thought that this place was a beginning and not an end. The thought did not have a date attached to it. It did not have a plan. It had only a direction. The direction has been with me ever since.

## What the row gave me

I left the row, in due course, the way the younger children of a long-serving miner are supposed to leave the row — not in any hurry, not with any announcement, but slowly and steadily and on the back of a trade test that the mine itself had paid for. The house, eventually, passed out of the family. My parents are buried, side by side, in a small cemetery I still visit when the road takes me back to the East Rand. The row of houses still stands, though the head-gear above Sub Nigel no longer turns the way it once did and the hooter no longer governs the day of the men who live in those rooms.

If I sit, now, at the age of seventy-three, and ask myself what the row actually gave me, the answer is not nostalgia. The row was not picturesque. It was a hard, plain, lower-middle-income environment in which a great deal of hidden labour was being done quietly by people whose names will not appear in any business book of mine. The row gave me three things that I have never been able to source from anywhere else, and that I have spent fifty-seven working years trying to repay.

The first thing it gave me was a working definition of duty. My father went down on his shift because the shift required it of him, and because the Lord, in his account of the world, required it of him as well. He did not need to be motivated. He did not need to be inspired. He needed only to be reminded, by the hooter, that the time had come. I have tried, in every plant I have ever managed, to model that posture for the younger engineers around me. The work is the work. The hooter, in whatever modern form it now takes — the ERP alarm, the email at six in the morning, the OEE screen on the wall — is the hooter. You go.

The second thing the row gave me was a working definition of stewardship. My mother ran a household budget that should not, on paper, have lasted a single month. It lasted decades. It clothed five children, fed five children, equipped five children with school books and church shoes, and still, somehow, kept a kettle warm in a kitchen at four in the morning for a working apprentice with a correspondence textbook open on the corner of the table. I have not, in any boardroom of my career, met a finance director who husbanded working capital with the seriousness that my mother husbanded a tin of polish. I have tried, in every business I have ever helped to transform, to bring some shadow of that seriousness back into the costing meetings.

The third thing the row gave me was a working definition of faith. Faith, on the row, was not a marketing instrument. It was not a Sunday performance. It was the orientation of the household, present in the way the family prayed before a meal, present in the way my father carried himself underground, present in the way my mother woke a sixteen-year-old at four in the morning and put a chair beside a kitchen table. The faith was operational. It ran in the background. It governed the order of the loyalties, in roughly the order I have already described — shaft and Lord on a weekday, Lord and shaft on a Sunday. I have, in every difficult decision of my working life, tried to keep that ordering alive. Some days I have done it better than others. The decision to leave mining for AECI, when the time came, was made inside that ordering. So was every retrenchment letter I have ever signed and every reference letter I have ever written for a person I have just let go.

I went into the mine as an apprentice at sixteen, and I went in with the full intention of going out again. The going in is the subject of the next chapter. The going out, eventually, is the subject of the chapter after that. But before any of those chapters could be written, there had to be a row of semi-detached houses on the East Rand, a hooter that governed the day, a father whose lamp was on a hook beside fifty others, a mother with a kettle on the stove at four in the morning, and a brother in oil-smelling overalls who had walked the path one full year ahead of me.

That row is where this book actually begins. Everything that comes afterwards is, in one way or another, an attempt to honour the people who lived on it.

existingChapters.title: Study Commencement: The Apprentice Who Refused to Stay One
existingChapters.summary: Tells the story of the apprenticeship at Sub Nigel, the mother who woke him before dawn to study, and the mine-sponsored engineering studies he pursued alongside his trade. Builds to passing the trade test at nineteen, becoming a qualified Boilermaker Artisan, and at twenty becoming the youngest artisan on the mine and the first Boilermaker ever to earn a Mechanical Engineering Diploma there, with his father called up from underground to witness the ceremony.

existingChapters.title: Early Career and the Move to AECI
existingChapters.summary: Covers the transfer to Libanon Gold Mine as Engineer-in-Training, marriage to Antonette at twenty-two, the birth of their first three children, and the decision to leave mining for AECI in Sasolburg, partly driven by his father's death from lung disease. Captures the pressure and exposure of a 24/7 chemical plant, life as one of fifteen engineers, and the appointment as manager of the VCM plant that set up the German assignment.

# Early Career and the Move to AECI

There is a particular kind of light that falls on a gold mine in the late afternoon, when the day shift has come up and the evening shift is filing in, and the headgear throws a long shadow across the yard. I remember standing in that light one Friday in the early seventies, in clean overalls and a new hard hat, looking up at the wheels of the shaft and realising that the place where I had served my apprenticeship was about to become the place where I would learn to be an engineer. The trade had been honest work. The next step would have to be something else entirely.

The transfer to Libanon Gold Mine came through with very little ceremony. A letter from the company, a handshake from my section foreman at Sub Nigel, a quiet word from one of the older engineers who had taken an interest in my studies, and that was that. I packed my tools, signed the necessary papers, and presented myself at the new mine as an Engineer-in-Training. The job title had three words in it where the apprenticeship had only had one, and I felt the weight of every additional syllable.

## A Different Pair of Boots

The difference between artisan work and engineer-in-training work is not, as I once thought, a difference of skill. It is a difference of accountability. As a Boilermaker on the floor, I had been measured by the quality of what came off the bench: a weld that held, a bracket that ran true, a repair that did not need to be redone on the next shift. As an Engineer-in-Training, I was being measured by something less tangible and considerably more uncomfortable. I was being measured by whether the things I planned actually worked when other men, in other overalls, eventually built them.

That shift in accountability was an education in itself. I learned, in those first months at Libanon, that an engineer's job is not to know more than the artisan. It is to be answerable for the consequences of decisions the artisan cannot reasonably be expected to make alone. The drawing has to be right. The specification has to be right. The sequencing has to be right. And if it is not, the man on the spanner end of the job is the one who pays for it in sweat and lost hours, while the man on the drawing-board end goes home to his supper.

I was determined, even then, never to be the man who went home easily while other men were still paying for my mistakes.

## Marriage at Twenty-Two

In the middle of all of this, in the middle of Libanon and the long evening classes and the early-morning shifts and the slow accumulation of mining knowledge that no diploma can teach you, I married Antonette. I was twenty-two years old. She was, by any reasonable measure, the better part of the bargain.

I will not pretend that I knew, at twenty-two, what marriage actually was. Nobody does, no matter what they tell you on their wedding day. What I knew, and what I have come to be grateful for in every decade since, is that I had married a woman whose centre was not me. Her centre was her faith, and from that centre everything else found its proper place. The household, the children when they came, the patience with my hours, the quiet, unsentimental insistence that the family would pray together on a Sunday no matter what the work week had cost us. All of that came out of a still point that had nothing to do with me and everything to do with the God she trusted.

That has made all the difference. A career like mine, with its travel and its plant emergencies and its years of being too tired at the dinner table to be useful company, would have ended a marriage built on shallower ground. Ours was not shallow.

## Three Children in Quick Succession

The children came, as children do in a young marriage, faster than the salary increases that were supposed to accommodate them. Our first three arrived inside the early Libanon years, and the small house we lived in began to feel as if it were inhaling them rather than the other way around. There were cots in corners and prams in passageways and, for a long stretch, a permanent low background hum of small voices that I have since come to miss in a way I never expected to.

Antonette carried most of it. I will not dress that up. I was on shift, or at lectures, or at the drawing-board, or in the workshop trying to coax some piece of obstinate mining gear back to life, while she was at home with babies who had no understanding of mining schedules. She did it with a steadiness that I, with all my engineering training, could not have replicated for a single weekend. I learned, in those years, that the household is its own kind of plant. It has its own throughput, its own bottlenecks, its own quiet maintenance regime. And, like every plant, it runs on the discipline of whoever is responsible for it when nobody senior is watching. In our house that was Antonette, and the plant ran.

## A Father Who Came Up for the Last Time

The decision to leave mining did not arrive as a single moment of clarity. It arrived, as so many important decisions do, in stages, the way a fever rises. The first stage was the lung disease that had begun, almost imperceptibly, to bend my father.

He had spent the working life of a man underground at Sub Nigel. He had gone down with his lamp and come up with his cough, year after year, decade after decade, and never once complained that the bargain had been a poor one. My father was not a complaining man. He was a churchgoing, plain-spoken miner who believed that work was a duty and that duty did not have to be enjoyable to be honourable. He believed in his shaft and in his Lord, in roughly that order on a weekday and in the reverse order on a Sunday, and he had raised five of us on those two beliefs.

He died at sixty-three. The disease that took him was the disease that had taken many of his shift-mates before him. I had grown up watching old miners cough into handkerchiefs and apologise for the noise. I had grown up knowing, in the way a child knows things without admitting them, that the air in those shafts was not entirely the same air the rest of the country breathed.

When the doctors confirmed what the cough had already announced, I made a decision that, on paper, looked like a career choice and, in fact, was a son's promise. I would not spend my working life underground. I owed my father too much to make the same trade he had made. The mine had given me my trade test and my engineering diploma and my first appointment as an Engineer-in-Training, and I would never speak ill of it for that. But it had also taken my father, and I had three small children now, and I was not going to ask Antonette to live the life my mother had lived, with a husband whose lungs were slowly being audited by the rock.

I began, quietly, to look for work above ground.

It is worth saying, because I have heard the question put to me more than once over the years, that this was not a rejection of the mine. The mine had been good to me. It had paid me through my trade test, sponsored my engineering studies, watched my father witness my graduation, and given me my first engineering appointment. I owed it a debt I have never tried to deny. But debts and decisions are not the same currency. You can be grateful to an institution and still know, in the part of yourself that is responsible for the next thirty years of your family's life, that the next chapter has to be written somewhere else. I was grateful, and I was leaving.

## AECI, Sasolburg

The opportunity that came was at AECI in Sasolburg. For an engineer in that period, AECI was a serious employer in a serious country town: a big chemicals operation in a corner of the then Orange Free State that had been purpose-built around heavy industry. The plant ran twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week, three hundred and sixty-five days a year, the way a chemical plant has to run if the economics of its first cubic metre of feedstock are going to make any sense at all.

I accepted the offer with a mixture of relief and apprehension. The relief was easy to understand. I would be out of the shaft for good, and the children would grow up with a father whose lungs had a fair chance of seeing them married. The apprehension was less articulate and, in retrospect, more useful. I had no chemical-plant experience. I had spent my engineering years on rock-breakers and winch motors and steel structures, not on reactors and distillation columns and the unforgiving chemistry of monomers. I was about to learn an entirely new industry while still pretending, in front of fifteen other engineers, that I belonged in the room.

Sasolburg, in those years, was a town that ran on the rhythm of the plants around it. You could tell the time by the shift change. You could tell the season by the colour of the veld between the perimeter fences. The houses were neat, the lawns were green, and the wives knew each other through the school and the church and the shared understanding that their husbands' beepers were going to go off at the worst possible moments.

We moved Antonette and the three children into one of those houses, and we joined the small congregation up the road, and we began.

## Fifteen Engineers and a 24/7 Plant

A 24/7 chemical plant is not a workplace. It is a living organism, and an engineer's first job is to learn its temper. There were fifteen of us in the engineering complement, each responsible for a slice of the operation, each with a pager that did not respect dinner times or christenings or sleep. The plant did not know that Tuesday at half past three in the morning was an unreasonable hour to call an engineer out of bed. The plant only knew its set points and its alarms, and when one of those went the wrong way, somebody in overalls had to be standing in front of it inside the hour.

I learned, in those first eighteen months at AECI, three things that I have carried into every plant I have ever run since.

- A chemical plant rewards engineers who arrive on site before they have to. The man who is already pulling on his overalls in the change-room when the second alarm goes is the man who saves the batch. The man who is still in his car, three kilometres out, is the man who explains the lost batch to the production director at eight the next morning.
- A 24/7 operation has no patience with personal calendars. Either your domestic life is constructed in such a way that it can absorb a plant emergency at any hour, or it is not, and a great deal of difficulty in young engineers' marriages comes from pretending that it is when it is not. I had Antonette. Many of my colleagues did not have a partner of that calibre, and I watched, with quiet sadness, what the pager did to some of those households.
- An engineer in a chemical plant earns his stripes not by knowing the chemistry better than the chemists do, but by understanding, in his bones, what failure looks like before it fails. A leaking flange that is not quite a leak yet. A pump bearing that is half a decibel louder than it was yesterday. A reactor temperature that is climbing in a way that the trend chart has not yet thought to alarm. Those are the things that separate the engineer who runs a plant from the engineer who is merely employed by one.

I was determined to be the first kind. I made it my private discipline, in those Sasolburg years, to walk the section I was responsible for at least once on every shift I was on, even when the shift was quiet. I wanted my plant to know my footsteps before it knew my voice on the radio.

## The VCM Plant

The appointment that changed the shape of my AECI years came when I was made manager of the VCM plant — the vinyl chloride monomer operation that sat at the heart of the site's downstream chemistry. VCM is not a forgiving product. It is a feedstock for PVC manufacture, and the conditions under which it is produced require respect of a kind that a younger engineer can take a while to acquire. The temperatures, the pressures, the toxicity, the explosion limits, the catalyst handling — every variable in the operation had a way of punishing inattention.

Being given that plant to manage was, in the politest possible language, a test. AECI did not put men into the VCM seat to see what happened. They put them in to see whether they would still be there in eighteen months with the plant running at or above nameplate and with a safety record they could defend in front of a board.

I gave the plant everything I had. I built a routine of walking the unit at the start of every shift I worked, including the night shifts that nobody senior was ever going to inspect. I learned the names of the operators on each crew, and their wives' names where I could, and the names of the children they were trying to put through school on shift allowance. I sat in on the toolbox talks. I read the shift logs at the end of every week, line by line, until the patterns of small abnormalities began to form a kind of map in my head. I learned, in the VCM years, that a plant tells you what is wrong with it long before it tells you in the form of a trip, if you are prepared to listen at the level of small abnormalities rather than at the level of headline alarms.

The plant performed. The board, in due course, noticed.

One small habit from those VCM months has stayed with me through every plant I have run since. I started keeping a private notebook — nothing official, nothing the company ever saw — in which I wrote, at the end of every week, the three things I had nearly missed. Not the things I had caught and corrected, which any production report would already have captured, but the things I had almost not caught: the gauge I had walked past twice before noticing the drift, the operator complaint I had nearly dismissed, the trend I had almost rationalised away. Reading those notebooks back, six months at a time, taught me more about how plants actually fail than any safety course I have ever attended. Plants do not fail because engineers are stupid. Plants fail because engineers, including very good ones, learn to look at the wrong thing first.

## A Telephone Call from Germany

The conversation that ended this chapter of my life began with a telephone call I was not expecting. AECI had a relationship with a German technology partner, and a project of some significance was being scoped on the other side of the world. A South African engineer was wanted on site, for a period long enough to take a family for, in a role technical enough to matter. My name had been put forward.

I will write properly about Germany when it is the right moment to write about it. For the purposes of this chapter, it is enough to say that the call came, and that the offer was put on the table, and that I went home that evening to Antonette and our three children and laid the offer in front of her the way you lay an instrument in front of a craftsman: not to ask her permission, exactly, but to ask whether she could see what I could see in it. She could. She always could.

## What Sasolburg Taught Me

I left the mining industry as an Engineer-in-Training, full of borrowed authority and untested theory. I left Sasolburg, several years later, as a manager of one of the most demanding plants in the country, with a habit of walking my unit before anyone asked me to, and a marriage that had not only survived a 24/7 environment but had quietly hardened into the partnership that would carry the rest of my career.

If I had to compress what those AECI years gave me into a single sentence, it would be this. A plant teaches you, more thoroughly than any classroom ever can, that the price of operational excellence is paid in advance, in the small disciplines of attention, and never in arrears, in the loud heroics of recovery. The engineers who tried to pay it in arrears burned out, or buried their reputations under one too many lost batches, or, in the most painful cases I watched, buried their marriages instead. The engineers who paid it in advance, in the long, unglamorous, walk-the-line, read-the-logs, learn-the-operators' currency of preparation, were the ones who eventually got the German telephone calls.

I had paid it in advance. The German telephone call had come. And on the East Rand, in a small cemetery I still visited when I could, the miner who had spent his working life underground at Sub Nigel had, in his quiet way, helped me decide which kind of engineer I was going to be.

existingChapters.title: Manufacturing Discipline: GMP, Poka-Yoke and Pharmaceutical Rigour
existingChapters.summary: Explores the discipline of manufacturing products that can kill people if mishandled: validations, housekeeping, food and pharma safety standards, and the Japanese 'fool-proof' Poka-Yoke philosophy that became a permanent operating instinct. existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Manufacturing Discipline: GMP, Poka-Yoke and Pharmaceutical Rigour

There is a particular kind of quiet that settles over a pharmaceutical packing hall in the small hours of the morning, when the lines are running and the operators have found their rhythm and the only sound left is the soft, regular percussion of bottles meeting their caps. It is not the quiet of a workshop after hours. It is the quiet of a place where, on every shift, men and women are making products that, if they leave the building in the wrong condition, can kill the person who eventually swallows them. I had not understood, when I left the cyanide and peroxide plants of AECI for the consumer-pharmaceuticals world of Adcock Ingram, that I was crossing into an industry whose first commandment was not productivity, and not even safety in the ordinary sense, but a discipline so much older and so much more unforgiving that it had its own three-letter shorthand: GMP.

Good Manufacturing Practice. Three words that sound, when you first hear them, like the kind of slogan a chairman puts on a calendar. Three words that, when you live inside them for fourteen years, become the operating instinct of every decision you make for the rest of your career.

## A different category of consequence

I had come into Adcock from a heavy-chemicals world in which the consequences of a mistake were dramatic, immediate and, in their own grim way, easy to understand. A reactor overpressured. A flange leaked. A trip closed the plant. The damage was loud and the diagnosis was honest. You could see it, you could smell it, and the trend chart on the control-room wall would tell you, after the fact, exactly when in the previous twenty-four hours the failure had begun to assemble itself.

Pharmaceutical manufacturing is not like that. The consequence of a mistake in a pharmaceutical plant can be invisible for weeks, or months, or in the worst cases years. A blister pack that has been overfilled by one tablet. A label that has been printed on the wrong reel of foil. A sterile fill that has lost integrity for ninety seconds inside an eight-hour shift. A solvent residue that is one part per million above its specification on a single batch out of eighty. None of those failures announces itself in the room they occur in. They announce themselves later, in a patient's bathroom, in a doctor's surgery, in a regulator's letter, or, on the days I never want to live through again, in a recall.

That is the category of consequence a pharmaceutical engineer is paid to prevent. And the moment you have understood that, you understand why GMP is not a paperwork exercise and not a slogan on a calendar. GMP is the institutional memory of every mistake the industry has ever made, written down in such a way that no plant has to make it twice.

## The discipline of writing it down

The first thing that struck me, in my early months as Company Engineer at Adcock, was how much of the work consisted of writing.

I had come from plants where the engineer's job was, in essence, to keep things running and to keep people safe while they did so. The writing happened, of course, but it happened around the work. In pharmaceuticals the writing was the work. Every procedure was documented. Every change was controlled. Every deviation was investigated. Every batch had a record that could be unrolled, years later, in front of an auditor, and that had to tell, line by signed line, the entire story of how a particular bottle of cough syrup had come to be a bottle of cough syrup.

I will admit that, in the beginning, it irritated me. I had grown up on the principle that an engineer earns his stripes on the plant floor and not at a desk, and the sheer volume of paper that pharmaceutical manufacture demanded felt, for a while, like a tax on real work. It took me a year of audits and investigations and one or two genuinely frightening near-misses to understand that the paper was not a tax on the work. The paper was the work. An undocumented change in a pharmaceutical plant is not a change. It is an unauthorised alteration of a product that human beings are about to put inside their bodies, and the discipline of writing it down is the discipline that separates a manufacturer from an experimenter.

Once I had absorbed that, the writing stopped irritating me. It started teaching me. The change-control log of a well-run pharmaceutical plant is, if you read it patiently, a textbook on how the plant actually thinks. The deviation register is a textbook on where its weaknesses are. The batch records are a textbook on the consistency, or otherwise, of the people running the lines. I learned, in those Adcock years, that an engineer who cannot read his own plant's paperwork is an engineer who is, in the end, working in someone else's plant.

## Validation: proving the obvious

The other shock of those first months was validation.

In a chemicals plant, you commission a unit, you run it, you tune it, and you move on. In a pharmaceutical plant, you validate it. You prove, with documented evidence, that the equipment does what you say it does, that the process produces what you say it produces, and that the product meets specification not once but consistently, batch after batch, under the conditions you have committed to. You do this before the first commercial batch leaves the door. You do it again whenever anything material changes. And you do it, when you are honest with yourself, with a small part of your mind constantly asking whether the next inspector is going to find something you did not.

The first validation exercise I led at Adcock was a packing-line redesign that, in any other industry, would have been signed off in an afternoon. In pharmaceuticals it took the better part of three months. I had to write the protocol. I had to argue, line by line, why each acceptance criterion was the right one. I had to run the line under nominal conditions, under stressed conditions, and under the worst-case combination of conditions the protocol could justify. I had to record every reading, sign every page, and explain every anomaly. By the end of it I had a fat lever-arch file, a vague sense that I had aged a year in a quarter, and an understanding of validation that no textbook could have given me.

Validation, I came to see, is simply the engineer's discipline of proving the obvious. It is the formal admission that the obvious, in a regulated industry, is not allowed to remain merely obvious. It has to be demonstrated, documented and defended. And the moment you accept that as a worldview, your relationship with your own plant changes. You stop trusting it. You start verifying it. And, paradoxically, you start running it better than you ever did when you trusted it.

## Housekeeping as evidence

There is a corollary to that worldview which I have carried into every plant I have run since, and it is this. In a pharmaceutical or food plant, housekeeping is not housekeeping. Housekeeping is evidence.

A regulator who walks into a packing hall and sees clean floors, marked walkways, labelled bins, dated stock, sequenced product changeovers and operators in clean PPE does not, in the first instance, congratulate the plant on its tidiness. He registers, in the part of his brain that is paid to register such things, that the plant is being run by people who understand what they are doing. Conversely, a regulator who walks into a hall and sees scattered offcuts, mixed allergens on adjacent lines, undated work-in-progress and labels stored on the floor does not, in the first instance, write the plant up for poor housekeeping. He registers that the plant is, in his professional judgement, an outbreak waiting to happen, and the rest of his audit is conducted from that assumption.

That was the lesson I absorbed at Adcock, and it is the lesson I took, line for line, into every food and consumer plant I subsequently ran. The 5S markings on the floor of a beverage plant. The colour-coded scoops in a peanut-butter line. The sterile zones, the high-care zones, the allergen segregation. The dated tape on the inside of a tank. The mop that has its own labelled hook, and a different colour for the cleaning of a different zone. None of these are aesthetic decisions. They are the visible vocabulary of a manufacturer telling its regulators, its customers and, most importantly, itself, that it knows the difference between a clean line and a compliant one.

## Poka-Yoke: the Japanese gift

The phrase I have used more often than any other in the second half of my career came to me, indirectly, from the Japanese.

Poka-Yoke. Mistake-proofing. The principle that a process should be designed in such a way that the wrong action cannot, physically or procedurally, be taken. A connector that only fits one way. A label that only feeds in one orientation. A bottle that cannot pass a fill checkweigher unless it is filled to a tolerance the line has already accepted. A door that cannot be opened during a high-care cycle without halting the line. A code on a carton that cannot be over-printed unless the master recipe and the printer's instruction agree.

The genius of Poka-Yoke, I have come to believe, is not its cleverness. The genius is its humility. It begins from the engineer's acceptance that human beings, including very good ones, including ones who have been on the line for twenty years, will, under fatigue or distraction or unusual conditions, sooner or later do the wrong thing. The instinctive Western engineering response to that fact is to train, to retrain, to discipline and, in due course, to blame. The Japanese response is older and wiser. It is to design the wrong thing out of the system, so that the line cannot make the mistake even if the operator, on his worst day, attempts it.

I learned Poka-Yoke first at Adcock, in the context of pharmaceutical packing, where the consequences of a label or batch-code error are very serious indeed. But it did not stay in pharmaceuticals. It became, over the decades that followed, the single principle I carried into every plant I ran, in every industry I touched. The mogul lines, the chocolate plants, the peanut-butter operation, the beverage filling halls, the food-supplement plant — every one of them was, at some level, a project to embed Poka-Yoke in the place where, before I arrived, training and exhortation had been doing the work that geometry and interlocks should have been doing.

That habit, I should say plainly, is what FSSC accreditation, BRC certification, and the rest of the food-safety alphabet are really measuring. They are not measuring how clean the plant looks on audit day. They are measuring whether the plant has been designed, in detail, so that the wrong outcome cannot happen on the day no auditor is present.

## The pharmaceutical mindset, ported to food

When I left Adcock, fourteen years later, and began to run food and consumer plants in the years that followed, I took the pharmaceutical mindset with me intact. I have sometimes been asked, in interviews and in classrooms, whether it is reasonable to apply pharmaceutical disciplines to a peanut-butter line or a sweet-manufacturing operation. My answer has not changed in three decades. It is not only reasonable. It is the cheapest insurance policy a food operator can buy.

A consumer who buys a jar of peanut butter does not read the batch record. She trusts, in the casual way consumers trust, that the jar in her trolley is what its label says it is, that it contains what its ingredients list says it contains, and that nothing in it will make her child ill. That trust is the entire commercial premise of the brand. And the only mechanism on earth that protects that trust, in the long run, is a manufacturing discipline that treats the jar with the same seriousness a pharmaceutical company treats a tablet.

In practice that meant several specific things, and I list them here not as theory but as the working principles I imported, plant after plant, into operations that had often never seen them.

- Every critical process step had to have a documented procedure, signed off, version-controlled, and present, in laminated form, at the station where the step was performed. If the procedure was not at the station, the step did not exist.
- Every change to a process, however small, had to pass through a written change-control protocol. The line operator who tightens a screw on his own initiative is, in a regulated environment, an unmanaged risk. The change-control register is what converts that initiative into a managed improvement.
- Every deviation, however apparently trivial, had to be investigated to root cause and closed out with a documented corrective and preventive action. A deviation closed with the words "operator error" is a deviation that has not been investigated. People are almost never the root cause. The system that allowed the person to fail is.
- Every line had to be designed, wherever the budget allowed, with Poka-Yoke at the level of geometry rather than at the level of attention. Where geometry could not deliver it, sensors and interlocks had to. Where sensors and interlocks could not deliver it, the procedure had to be written in such a way that the wrong action triggered an audible, witnessed stop.
- Every plant had to be walked, by its accountable manager, in the first hour of every shift. Not the morning shift. Every shift. The plant tells you what is going wrong with it long before the trend chart does, and the manager who is not on his lines at the start of each shift is, in my experience, the manager who is going to read about his own failures in a recall letter.

Those five principles are not, in themselves, novel. They are the everyday vocabulary of any decently run pharmaceutical operation. What was novel, in my career, was the patient business of carrying them across the industry boundary, into food and consumer manufacture, and demonstrating that they paid their own way in lower wastage, lower recall risk, higher OEE and a reputation with customers that no marketing budget could have bought.

## What the auditors really came for

In the Adcock years I sat through a great many audits. Internal audits, corporate audits, customer audits, regulator audits. The auditors changed. The questions did not.

What they came to see, underneath the paperwork, was very simple. They came to see whether the plant was being run by people who were honest about its weaknesses. A plant that pretends, on audit day, to have no deviations is a plant that is lying. A plant that hands the auditor a deviation register with one hundred and thirty entries, every one investigated, every one closed, every one trending toward a root cause that the plant is actively engineering out, is a plant whose auditor goes home satisfied.

I learned, in those years, to welcome auditors. I learned to give them the worst of my deviation register first, before they asked, and to walk them through what we had learned from each one. I learned that the manufacturer who tries to hide his weaknesses from a competent auditor is a manufacturer who is going to be found out, sooner or later, by a less forgiving instrument than an auditor's report. The market. The regulator. The recall. In that order of severity.

The habit of honesty about one's own plant, I have come to think, is the real moat under any sustainable manufacturing operation. Everything else — the equipment, the recipes, the people, the certifications — can be bought, copied or imitated. The discipline of looking, week after week, at one's own deviation register and asking what it is trying to teach is the one thing competitors cannot reverse-engineer.

## A permanent operating instinct

I left Adcock, in due course, for the next chapter of my working life, and I have written elsewhere about what came next. What I want to record here, while the subject is on the table, is what those fourteen years gave me that has never left.

They gave me a permanent operating instinct. The instinct to walk a plant looking for what is not quite right rather than for what is going well. The instinct to read a deviation register before I read a production report. The instinct to ask, of every new line, where its Poka-Yoke is and what it is doing on the day the most experienced operator is on leave. The instinct to treat housekeeping as evidence. The instinct to treat validation as the formal proof of the obvious. The instinct to be, in front of any auditor, a great deal more honest about my plant's weaknesses than the auditor was likely to have expected.

That instinct, I want to say plainly to any younger engineer who happens to be reading this, is not a personality trait. It is not a gift. It is the residue of fourteen years spent inside an industry that would not let me make a single decision casually, and it can be acquired by anyone who is willing to serve the same apprenticeship. You do not have to spend your career in pharmaceuticals to inherit it. You only have to spend long enough inside a properly regulated operation to understand, in your bones, that the price of consumer trust is paid in advance, in the small disciplines of attention, and never in arrears, in the loud heroics of recall management.

I had paid that price, by the end of my Adcock years, in a currency the rest of my career would continue to draw on. And when, some time later, a chairman in Port Elizabeth picked up the telephone with an offer that would change the shape of my working life again, the engineer he was calling was not the Engineer-in-Training who had once stood under the headgear at Sub Nigel. He was a manufacturing man, formed by GMP, fluent in Poka-Yoke, allergic to undocumented change, and quietly certain that the discipline of running a regulated plant was the discipline that would run anything.

existingChapters.title: Exports, Setbacks and the Confectionery Years
existingChapters.summary: Tracks the return to Adcock Ingram as Director of Exports into Africa, Europe and the Far East, the consolidation of Adcock, Tiger Brands and Beacon Sweets into one export basket, sales into Cyprus and Greece, and the eventual throttling of the export business by local-market capacity. Then covers the Retailer Brands transformation, the difficult Candy Tops contract in East London, the Candy Tops / Mr Sweet merger, and the harsh lesson of operating inside a loss-making, thin-margin confectionery business.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Exports, Setbacks and the Confectionery Years

There is a particular kind of homecoming that has nothing sentimental about it. After the SA Oil Mills exit and the quiet return of the family from Port Elizabeth to Gauteng, I sat at the kitchen table one Saturday morning and listed the offers in front of me. The list was short, but one name on it carried weight I could not ignore: Adcock Ingram. I had given fourteen years to that company once before. I had walked out as a Technical Director. And now they were asking me to come back, not into the manufacturing role I had built my reputation on, but into something I had circled the edges of my whole career — exports.

I said yes before the end of the weekend.

## Back Through the Same Gate

Walking back through the Adcock Ingram gate as Director of Exports felt like opening a familiar book at a chapter I had never read. The site smelled the same. Half the security guards greeted me by name. A few of the engineers I had hired in the late nineties were now seasoned managers in their own right, and one or two had stories about machines I had personally signed off on that were still running, fifteen years later, exactly as we had commissioned them. There is a quiet pride in that — in seeing your old work still earning its keep long after the contract has ended.

But this time, the brief was different. Adcock Ingram, Tiger Brands and Beacon Sweets were no longer to be treated as independent export islands. The group strategy had matured. Three product portfolios — over-the-counter pharmaceuticals, packaged foods and confectionery — were to be consolidated into a single export basket and pushed out through one coordinated office. I was that office.

On paper it looked obvious. In practice, very few large South African groups had managed to make a unified export programme work. Each business unit had its own pricing logic, its own distributor relationships, its own packaging conventions and, most importantly, its own marketing director with very firm views on what should and should not be sold beyond our borders. My first ninety days were less about exports and more about diplomacy.

## The Logic of a Unified Basket

I called it the basket strategy, partly because it sounded less corporate than "portfolio consolidation" and partly because I wanted the salespeople to picture it literally. A basket is what a distributor carries into the back of a shop. If our distributors could carry one basket with Adcock cough syrup, Tiger Brands maize meal and Beacon sweets all inside it, the cost per kilometre of selling collapsed. One container could leave Durban with three brand families inside it. One invoice could clear three sets of marketing budgets. One relationship in Lagos or Lusaka could open three doors.

The arithmetic was clean. The execution was anything but.

We began with the African corridor because the regulatory environment and the cultural familiarity made it the obvious place to test the system. Botswana, Namibia, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Mozambique and into East Africa — each market had its own quirks, but the basic logistics were within reach. From there, we pushed north into West Africa, where Nigeria and Ghana, despite their well-known port frustrations, offered scale that justified the operational pain.

Then I turned my attention to a smaller market that surprised everyone, including me: Cyprus. A Cypriot importer had taken an interest in our cough syrups and our boiled sweets at one of the European trade fairs. I flew out for what I expected to be a two-day visit and ended up staying nearly a week, walking through pharmacies and small grocery chains, watching how Mediterranean shoppers picked up an over-the-counter product, weighed it in their hand, read the back, and made their choice. The visit gave us our first beachhead in Southern Europe. From Cyprus, the trail led naturally to Greece, where the same distributor had family connections, and where a small but steady volume of cough preparations, antacids and confectionery began to move every quarter.

## Lessons in Negotiating Across Cultures

Anyone who tells you that international business is just domestic business with a longer flight has never sat across a table from a Greek pharmaceutical wholesaler at ten o'clock at night, drinking ouzo and being told, very politely, that the price you quoted on Tuesday morning is now considered an opening bid. Cyprus and Greece taught me three lessons I carried into every export deal that followed.

The first was patience as a pricing weapon. In African and European markets alike, the buyer who is most desperate for a deal usually loses the most margin. I taught my export team to walk into every meeting with two prices in their head — the price we wanted, and the price we would still walk away from happily — and to refuse to feel pressured by the dinner, the hospitality or the warmth of the host. Warm hospitality is a wonderful thing, but it does not pay the freight bill back to Johannesburg.

The second lesson was the importance of the local partner. In every market where we eventually built durable share, there was a local distributor whose name we trusted more than our own contract. The Cypriot family who carried us into Greece were not just our customers; over time they became our scouts, our translators and our risk filter. When they told us a payment term was unrealistic for a particular sub-region, we listened. When they told us a competitor was about to enter, we acted.

The third lesson was about packaging. South African export packs almost always had to be redesigned for international shelves. Languages, regulatory marks, barcodes, child-safety closures, expiry-date formats, font sizes for elderly readers — every market had its own quiet rules, and getting any one of them wrong could mean a full container sitting in customs for six weeks. I learned to start every new market by asking, not "Can we sell here?", but "Can we print here?"

## The Wall We Could Not Push Through

For about two and a half years the basket strategy delivered. Exports across the consolidated group grew in double digits year on year. We doubled our African footprint. We held the Cyprus and Greece volumes steady. We even ran a small pilot into the Middle East. The board liked the trend line. So did I.

And then, slowly, we ran into the wall that no exporter wants to discuss publicly but which kills more export programmes than any tariff or exchange rate: domestic capacity.

The Adcock manufacturing sites were busy. The Tiger Brands plants were busy. Beacon's confectionery lines were busy. When a domestic order from a South African retailer collided with an export order from Lagos, the domestic order won every time. It had to. The domestic market was the cash engine. The local retail customers — the supermarket buyers who could place or remove a brand from a national chain with a single decision — were not going to be told that their stock was sitting on a ship to Mombasa.

I argued for protected production windows. I argued for dedicated export shifts. I built spreadsheets showing the marginal contribution of an export order versus a domestic one, especially when the rand was weak. I won some battles. But the structural truth was unchanged: in a country that had never built spare manufacturing capacity for its export ambitions, exports would always be the buffer. When the local plants got tight, exports got throttled.

I want to be honest about this. The throttling of the export business was not a personal defeat in the way a failed plant project would have been. The numbers were good. The relationships we built lasted. But the limit was real, and I had to make peace with it. There comes a moment in every transformation career when you must distinguish between the things you can fix and the things that are structural. Domestic capacity, in that group at that time, was structural.

## Pivoting Inwards: Retailer Brands

When the export ceiling became clear, I made a deliberate decision not to fight it any longer. Instead, I went to the executive committee with a different proposal: let me take the export discipline — the cost control, the packaging rigour, the customer-led product design — and turn it inwards. Let me run Retailer Brands.

For readers outside the consumer-goods world, Retailer Brands are the products you see on a supermarket shelf carrying the supermarket's own name rather than a national brand. They are usually cheaper, often manufactured by the same factories that make the branded equivalents, and they have become, over the last twenty years, one of the most important profit pools in modern retail. For a group like ours, with serious manufacturing depth, Retailer Brands were a natural fit — but only if you could run them on margins thinner than anything our branded business had ever tolerated.

I took the Retailer Brands portfolio through a transformation that, in style, was very similar to what I had done at SA Oil Mills. I walked the lines. I asked, for every product, the same blunt question: what is the cheapest way we can make this to the agreed specification without breaking any food-safety or pharmaceutical-quality rule? I rationalised SKUs. I renegotiated commercial terms with two of our largest retail customers. I tightened the packing-line changeover times. And I made a point, every quarter, of walking through the stores myself, looking at our products next to the branded equivalents and asking honestly whether the consumer was getting fair value.

Retailer Brands grew. Margins improved. And then a particular contract walked through the door that would teach me one of the harder lessons of my career.

## The Candy Tops Contract

Candy Tops was a confectionery business based in East London. They had won — or, more accurately, been chased by a major retailer until they accepted — a private-label contract that, on the volume numbers, looked attractive, but on the unit economics was wafer-thin. Our group had a strategic interest in extending its confectionery footprint, and Candy Tops looked like the right vehicle.

I was sent down to East London to make it work.

I arrived expecting the familiar transformation playbook to apply. It did not. The plant was undercapitalised. The packaging lines were old and slow. The workforce was loyal but had never been exposed to the kind of operational discipline I had brought into other sites. And the contract itself was harsh: the retailer had negotiated a price that left almost no headroom for inefficiency. Every percentage point of waste, every changeover delay, every quality reject came directly off our margin.

I did what I knew how to do. I put OEE measurement in. I attacked the top three loss categories on the Pareto chart. I trained supervisors in basic shift-handover discipline. We made progress. Output rose. Waste came down. By any normal measure, it was a competent transformation.

But the contract itself was still poor. And here is the lesson I want to underline for any reader who one day finds themselves in a similar position: there are some commercial situations in which operational excellence cannot save you. If the price you have agreed to sell at is below the cost at which your industry can sustainably produce, no amount of factory discipline will close the gap. You will narrow it. You will narrow it impressively. But you will not close it.

I came back to head office and said so, plainly. I had been hired to transform operations, but I refused to let an operational story disguise a commercial one. The contract needed to be renegotiated, the volume needed to be cut, or the business needed to be merged into a larger confectionery footprint that could carry it. That was a hard conversation. It was the kind of conversation that, earlier in my career, I might have softened. By this point I had learned that softening the truth, in a loss-making business, only delays the bill.

## The Mr Sweet Merger and Its Aftermath

The third of those options became the path forward. Candy Tops was merged with Mr Sweet, a larger confectionery business with deeper category exposure, more sophisticated production technology and the kind of scale that could spread the East London plant's fixed costs across a broader revenue base. On paper, it was a sensible deal. The combined entity had a credible product range, real distribution and the prospect of becoming a meaningful player in the South African sweet market.

I stayed with the merged business for a period to see the integration through. We harmonised systems. We rationalised SKUs across the combined portfolio. We took a hard look at every plant in the combined footprint and ranked them on cost-to-produce. There was good operational work done in that period, and I am still proud of it.

But the harsh truth of the confectionery years did not go away. Sweet manufacturing in South Africa, like sweet manufacturing in many parts of the world, is a thin-margin business operating against retailer power, raw-material volatility — especially in sugar, glucose and cocoa — and consumer trends that increasingly pull spending towards healthier categories. We could optimise the factories. We could not change the structural economics of the category.

By the time my contract there ended, I had a private mental file labelled simply *Sweets — Lessons*. It was the most honest document I had written about my own work in years. I want to share its main entries.

## Lessons from the Confectionery Years

The first lesson was about contracts. Be very, very careful what you sign for. In a thin-margin business, the price you agree on day one is almost certainly the price you will be living with three years later, regardless of what the inflation rate does to your inputs. If a contract does not have a sensible mechanism to pass through raw-material movements, it is not a contract — it is a slow leak.

The second lesson was about scale. There is a minimum efficient scale below which a confectionery plant simply cannot compete, no matter how skilled the operators or how disciplined the supervisors. The East London plant, in isolation, was always going to struggle. Inside a larger group, with shared overheads and combined buying power, it had a fighting chance. Below that threshold, no transformation specialist on earth could have made it work sustainably.

The third lesson was about the limits of personal effort. I had spent my career proving that focused, analytical, hands-on work could turn around almost any operation. The confectionery years quietly amended that belief. Personal effort is necessary, but it is not sufficient. The commercial and structural conditions have to give you something to work with. When they do not, the honest thing to do is to name the structural problem out loud and propose a structural solution, even if it is uncomfortable.

The fourth lesson was about humility. Up to that point, every business I had touched had come out the other side stronger and, in most cases, more profitable. The confectionery years were the first time I had to look at a business I had personally led and admit that, although I had made it better, I had not made it good. That admission, in my own private notes, was harder to write than any board presentation I have ever given. But it was, in the end, the foundation of the work that followed at Tiger Brands and beyond.

## The Quiet Hand of Faith and Family

In the middle of all of this, the family stayed steady, as they always had. Antonette had by then become quite practised at the rhythm of my contract life — the long weeks away in East London, the late phone calls about a packing-line breakdown, the occasional Sunday flight back to Johannesburg in time for church. She did not complain about the rhythm. She did, however, remind me, in her quiet and unshowy way, that no contract was worth the children growing up without their father at the dinner table on the nights when I was in town.

I held to that. On the nights I was home, I was home. The briefcase stayed in the study. The phone went on silent. The lessons I was learning about commercial reality in East London were, in their own way, being matched by a parallel lesson at home: that the structural foundation under a long marriage is not romance, and not even shared history, but a thousand small choices to put the people in front of you ahead of the spreadsheet behind you.

When I look back at the export years and the confectionery years together, I do not see them as a low point. I see them as the period in which my transformation playbook grew up. Africa, Cyprus and Greece taught me how to sell across borders. The throttled-export ceiling taught me that not every problem is mine to fix. Candy Tops and Mr Sweet taught me to look at commercial structure before operational symptoms. And the road between East London and Johannesburg, driven and flown more times than I can count, taught me — once again, more deeply — that the family I was working for was also the family I had to be present for.

The next chapter of my contract life was already taking shape. Tiger Brands, in its full operational complexity, was about to ask me a much bigger question. And by the time it did, the lessons of the confectionery years would be exactly the equipment I needed to answer it well.

existingChapters.summary: Goes deep on the Tiger Brands contract years: the failed R200-million gums-and-jellies installation, fixing the mogul line in six months, the four-year stay and promotion to head supply chain for confectionery, the Pareto-driven 20/80 attack on root causes, then the fourteen-month transformation of candy and chocolate operations with 1400 wage and 40 management staff reporting in, the OEE real-time monitoring system, and finally the Black Cat peanut-butter raw-material problem solved and output dramatically lifted.
