# Tiger Brands Turnarounds: Mogul Lines, Chocolate and Peanut Butter

There is a particular phone call that arrives in the life of every transformation specialist sooner or later, and when it arrives you know, before the second sentence, that the company on the other end has finally run out of its own answers. The call that brought me to Tiger Brands came on a Tuesday morning. I remember it because I had spent the previous evening writing up the final notes from the Candy Tops and Mr Sweet integration, and I was sitting at my desk in Johannesburg in that quiet hour between an old contract and a new one, when the operating mind is at its most useful and the diary is at its emptiest.

The voice on the line was a senior executive at Tiger Brands. He came to the point with the kind of directness that I have always preferred. There was a plant in the confectionary group, he said, that had received a capital installation of roughly two hundred million rand for a new gums-and-jellies line. The line had been commissioned. The line did not work. The volumes that had been promised to the board were not being produced. The chocolate and candy operations sitting alongside that line were tired, under-measured and below standard. The supply chain across the broader confectionary business was not what the group expected of it. Would I come and see them.

I went and saw them. By the end of that meeting we had the outline of an agreement. I was to take responsibility for the failed mogul line first, restore it to producing volumes that the business plan had assumed, and then, once it was earning its keep, broaden my mandate across the rest of confectionary. The contract was set up in stages, as the best contracts always are. I would prove the first task. The second task would be mine to lose.

## A Two-Hundred-Million-Rand Machine That Would Not Run

For readers who have never stood in front of a gums-and-jellies installation, a brief description is in order, because the scale of it explains the seriousness of the problem. A modern mogul line is not a machine. It is a city block of equipment laid out end to end. Starches are conditioned and laid into tray after tray of moulded impressions. A heated liquid sweet mixture is deposited into those impressions to set. The trays are stacked, cured for hours in temperature-controlled rooms, returned, emptied, dusted, sorted, polished, sometimes sugared, sometimes oiled, and finally packed into the bags and jars that find their way onto a retailer's shelf. Every step has a tolerance. Every tolerance is small. And every variation at the front of the line compounds itself at the back, where the consumer pack is supposed to be uniform and the retailer's complaint book is supposed to stay empty.

The installation Tiger Brands had paid for was, on paper, world-class. The civil works had been done. The starch handling was in place. The depositors had been commissioned. The drying tunnels and the curing rooms had been built. Every piece of capital equipment that two hundred million rand can buy was sitting in that hall. And yet the line was producing a fraction of its nameplate, and on most days it was producing scrap.

I walked it the way I have always walked a plant. I did not write a report in the first fortnight. I did not promise the executive committee a date. I stood at each station, sometimes for an hour, and watched. I had lunches with the maintenance fitters. I read the commissioning logs that had been quietly filed away in cupboards. I asked the operators, who had been treated as the problem, what they would do if they were given the keys.

What I found was, in the end, not exotic. The line had been installed by competent contractors against a specification that was internally inconsistent. The starch-moisture range coming off the conditioning step did not match the depositor's preferred operating window. The cooling profile in the curing rooms had been set against an assumed ambient that the Gauteng summer did not respect. The changeover routines between products had not been written down by anybody, which meant that every shift improvised its own. The maintenance schedule had been copied from a sister plant in another country whose water and whose humidity bore no relation to ours. None of these were dramatic failures. Each of them was a few percentage points of efficiency. Stacked together, they were a plant that did not run.

I attacked them in the order in which they hurt us. We rewrote the starch conditioning specification first, because the rest of the line could not be settled until the front of the line behaved. We re-mapped the cooling profile to the conditions that actually existed in our curing rooms rather than the conditions a European brochure said should exist. We sat the operators down, in small groups, and wrote the changeover procedures together, so that the words on the laminated sheets matched the work being done by the hands. We rebuilt the maintenance schedule around our real failure modes, identified by reading two years of breakdown reports rather than by importing somebody else's PM schedule from another country. None of this was glamorous. All of it was necessary.

Six months from the day I walked through that gate, the mogul line was producing at the volumes the original business plan had committed to. The board, which had braced itself for a write-down on the asset, was quietly relieved. The executive who had hired me did not make a speech about it, which I appreciated, but he did do the thing that mattered more than a speech. He extended the contract.

## Promotion to Head of Supply Chain for Confectionary

When the mogul line had been steady for a full quarter, and the maintenance crew was running it without me standing behind them, the same executive came back to my office with a larger offer. The confectionary group as a whole was underperforming. The candy and chocolate operations sitting under the same roof as the gums-and-jellies plant were not in the same critical condition as the mogul line had been, but they were not what they needed to be either. The volumes were soft. The costs were drifting upwards. The quality reject numbers, on certain product families, were in the wrong place. Would I take responsibility for the supply chain across the entire confectionary business.

I accepted. I have always preferred to be measured against a broader mandate rather than a narrower one, because the broader the mandate the more honest the conversation about leverage. A plant manager can argue, sometimes correctly, that he is doing well within the constraints he has been given. A supply chain director cannot. The supply chain director is responsible for the constraints themselves.

That promotion turned a six-month brief into a four-year stay. By the time I closed out my first Tiger Brands contract I had been inside the group for forty-eight months, and I had developed an affection for the people in that confectionary business that has, in its quiet way, never quite left me. They were not the easiest workforce I have ever inherited. They were, in the end, one of the proudest I have ever led.

## The Twenty-Eighty of It

There is a principle that every operating mind eventually rediscovers, whether they have read Pareto in a textbook or whether they have simply worked in enough factories. Twenty percent of the causes drive eighty percent of the consequences. The mathematics is uneven by design. A few root causes are responsible for the great majority of the loss, and the great majority of root causes contribute only marginally. The instinct of the inexperienced manager, faced with a poorly performing plant, is to try to fix everything at once. The instinct of the experienced manager is to find the vital twenty percent and to attack it without distraction.

I drew a Pareto chart for every plant in the confectionary group inside my first quarter as supply chain director. Some of those charts were drawn on whiteboards. Some of them were printed and stuck on the wall outside the supervisor's office. All of them carried the same blunt question across the top: what are the three things that, if we fixed them this month, would deliver more than half of the available improvement.

The answers were rarely surprising once you saw them. In one plant it was changeover time on a single high-volume line. In another it was the reject rate on a particular packing format that nobody had bothered to track properly. In a third it was the unscheduled breakdowns on a single piece of equipment that had been limping along for years because the capital case to replace it had never quite made the cut. The discipline was not in finding the answer. The discipline was in refusing to be drawn into the other eighty percent of the problems until the twenty percent had been closed out.

I held that line as a leader. There are always people in a large business who want to spend a meeting talking about the problem they find most interesting rather than the problem that is doing the most damage. I have nothing against intellectual curiosity, but a confectionary business with thin margins cannot afford a leadership team that fights every battle. I made a habit, in those four years, of beginning every operating review with the same question. Which of our top three loss categories are we closing this month, and what is stopping us from closing them faster. The first time the question is asked, the room looks uncomfortable. The tenth time, the answers become specific. The hundredth time, the question has become the culture.

## The Fourteen-Month Transformation of Candy and Chocolate

The mogul line had been a contained problem. The candy and chocolate operations were a different kind of brief. The plants were larger, the workforces were bigger, the product families were more diverse, and the cultural inheritance was longer. I gave myself fourteen months to transform them, which on paper looked optimistic and in practice turned out to be the right window.

The numbers matter, because the numbers explain why the work was so demanding. I had roughly fourteen hundred wage staff reporting up through the supply chain and roughly forty management staff sitting between me and the lines. Fourteen hundred wage staff is not an abstraction. It is fourteen hundred people whose mortgages, families, school fees and Sunday plans depend on the decisions made in the head office. Forty management staff is not an abstraction either. It is forty individuals with their own histories in the business, their own ways of doing things, and their own quiet judgements about whether the new supply chain director was going to be worth listening to.

I have always believed that a transformation programme is, before it is anything else, a leadership programme. You can install every measurement system in the modern operating playbook, but if the supervisors on the floor do not believe in what you are doing, and if the wage staff behind them feel that they are being managed at rather than managed with, the measurements will be obeyed for a quarter and forgotten by the second year. The capital cost of putting in new systems is small. The leadership cost of making them stick is everything.

I began with the supervisors. I did not bring in consultants. I sat with them, in groups of six or eight, and I asked them what they would change in their own area if they had a free hand. I wrote their answers down. I read the answers back to them at the next session and asked them which of those changes they would take responsibility for delivering in the next ninety days. By the end of that first cycle of conversations I had a transformation programme that, in its essentials, had been written by the supervisors themselves, with my role being to remove the obstacles they could not remove for themselves.

The wage staff felt the shift quickly. People on a confectionary line know, very quickly, whether the senior managers are interested in them or interested only in the numbers. I made a point of walking every shift on every plant at least once in those first months. I stood at the wrapping machines. I sat in the cafeteria at lunch. I learned the names of the long-service operators and I learned the names of their children, in the way that one learns anything that matters, by asking and remembering. Within the first six months the air in the plants had changed, and you could feel it before any of the spreadsheets caught up.

The technical work, against that background, became simpler. We rationalised the product range on the highest-cost lines. We tightened the changeover routines, which in confectionary are everything because the recipes change often and the down time between recipes is where the money leaks. We attacked the reject rates one product family at a time, working from the top of the Pareto chart and refusing to be distracted by the categories below it. We installed proper shift-handover discipline, so that the early shift could not leave the line in a state that the late shift would have to spend the first hour repairing. We brought the food safety standards up to and beyond the audit thresholds, because in any branded confectionary business a single safety incident can do more damage to a brand than a year of poor service can do.

At the end of fourteen months the candy and chocolate operations were running differently. The volumes were where they were supposed to be. The costs had come down materially. The reject rates on the worst-performing product families had been more than halved. The workforce had a quiet pride in what they were producing. None of that was the work of one man, and I will not pretend otherwise. It was the work of fourteen hundred people who had decided, somewhere along the way, that they wanted their plants to be the best in the group.

## Putting OEE on the Wall

The technical centrepiece of all of this work, the single piece of equipment I trust more than any other in a manufacturing turnaround, was the real-time production monitoring system. We installed Overall Equipment Effectiveness measurement across the confectionary plants and we put the screens where every operator and every supervisor could see them. Not in a control room behind a locked door. Not on a manager's desktop computer in an office down the corridor. On the wall at the end of the line, in numbers large enough to be read across the floor, updated in real time.

I have, in the course of my career, watched many factories try to argue with their own performance. Once the OEE numbers are on a screen at the end of the line, the argument ends. The machine tells you what it is doing. The operators see it. The supervisors see it. The plant manager sees it. The site director sees it. The conversation, which until that moment had been about blame and explanation, becomes a conversation about improvement. Where did we lose the ten minutes between half past two and quarter to three. Why is this product family running below its standard rate. What is the changeover taking longer than we agreed last month. The questions become precise, and precise questions get specific answers, and specific answers can be acted on.

The OEE work was not glamorous. It was, in the truest sense, the kind of operating discipline that does not photograph well for an annual report. But by the time my first Tiger Brands contract ended, that monitoring system had become embedded in the culture of every plant in the confectionary group, and the supervisors who used it had stopped thinking of it as a new initiative and started thinking of it as the way work was done.

## Black Cat and the Lesson of the Peanut

The last problem of that first Tiger Brands era arrived from outside the confectionary business altogether. The Black Cat peanut butter plant, in Randfontein, was misbehaving. Outputs were below where they should have been. Quality reject rates were stubborn. The technical team had been chasing the symptoms for months. The executive committee asked whether, in the time I had left on my contract, I would take a look.

I went out to Randfontein the following week.

Peanut butter is a deceptively complicated product. To a consumer it is one ingredient, ground and salted, perhaps sweetened. To a manufacturer it is a chemistry experiment that depends on the raw material more than most processors are willing to admit. The peanut comes in graded by size, by oil content, by moisture, by the proportion of broken kernels, by the residual aflatoxin position, by the season, by the supplier and by the field. Get the wrong peanut and there is no roasting profile in the world that will rescue the product downstream.

When I walked the Black Cat line I could see, within a few hours, that the engineering team had been trying to fix a raw-material problem with manufacturing tools. They had been adjusting screws on grinders. They had been reworking roasting profiles. They had been studying the maintenance logs of the depositing equipment. All of that work was honest, and all of it was beside the point. The peanut coming through the goods-receiving doors was variable, and a variable peanut produces a variable peanut butter no matter what the downstream machinery is asked to do.

I worked the problem at its source. We tightened the incoming specifications. We changed the way we tested peanuts at the goods-receiving stage, with the testing now being done before the load was tipped rather than after. We worked with the procurement team to put pressure on suppliers whose deliveries had been inconsistent for years, and in one or two cases we changed suppliers altogether. Inside the plant we installed the same OEE measurement system that had become standard across the confectionary group, so that the operators could see, in real time, what the machinery was doing once the raw material had been brought into specification.

The improvement came faster than even I expected. Outputs lifted dramatically. The quality reject position stabilised and then improved. The plant that had been a quiet headache for the executive committee became, within a few months, a plant the executive committee no longer had to discuss. There is no greater compliment a transformation specialist can receive than to have his work removed from the operating review agenda because it has stopped being a problem.

## What That First Tiger Brands Era Taught Me

When my four-year contract ended, I closed it on the high note that I have insisted on for myself in every assignment of my contract years. The mogul line was running. The candy and chocolate plants were transformed. The OEE screens were on the walls and being watched. The Black Cat plant was producing. The handover documentation was clean. The supervisors I had developed were stepping into the roles they had earned.

The lessons of those four years have stayed with me, and it is worth setting them down here, plainly, because they have shaped every contract I have signed since.

The first lesson was that capital is not the same as capability. A two-hundred-million-rand installation that does not run is a more expensive problem than a smaller installation that does. The accountants will tell you that the capital has been spent and cannot be recovered, which is true. The operating mind will tell you that the only thing worse than a capital project that has failed is a capital project that has failed and been left in its failed state. Restoration is always cheaper than replacement. But restoration requires that somebody be willing to walk the line, station by station, and find the inconsistencies that the original commissioning missed.

The second lesson was that the Pareto chart is the most undervalued tool in modern management. It is not new. It is not fashionable. It does not photograph well in a consulting report. But it is the single most reliable instrument I know for keeping a leadership team focused on the twenty percent of the work that delivers eighty percent of the improvement. Every operating review I have run since those Tiger Brands years has begun with the same question, asked in the same way, until the question itself has become the culture.

The third lesson was that the people are the system. I had walked into a confectionary business with fourteen hundred wage staff and forty managers, and I had walked out with the same fourteen hundred wage staff and most of the same forty managers, but the business they were running had been transformed. The capital had not changed. The buildings had not changed. The machinery, in the main, was the machinery I had inherited. What had changed was the way the people in those plants worked together, watched their own performance, and held each other to a standard. That was not a piece of equipment I had installed. It was a habit I had spent four years building.

The fourth lesson was about raw materials. I have stood in many factories where the conversation about poor performance focused entirely on the downstream machinery. Black Cat reminded me, in a way I have never forgotten, that the most expensive variable in any food plant is the variable that walks through the goods-receiving door before the first machine has even been switched on. If your raw material is wrong, no amount of downstream skill will save you. If your raw material is right, even modest machinery can produce a product you can be proud of.

The fifth lesson, and perhaps the one that has mattered most to me as a leader, was that finishing on a high note is not a sentimental gesture. It is the foundation of the next contract. I had walked out of Tiger Brands with the standards higher than when I had arrived, with the people better trained than I had found them, and with the books in better shape than they had been on the day I signed. That habit had cost me nothing extra in those four years. The work to leave on a high note was the same work I would have done anyway. But the consequence of that habit was a phone, sitting in my study, that would continue to ring for years afterwards.

I did not know it on the morning I packed up that office. But the executive who had hired me would, within a few years, pick up that phone himself, and he would do so from a beverages company that needed a hand it remembered. The lessons of the first Tiger Brands era were, in that sense, not the end of a chapter. They were the opening line of the one that followed.

existingChapters.title: BEVCO, Future Life and Still Going at Seventy-Three
existingChapters.summary: Captures the late-career contract phase: BEVCO beverages, the return to Tiger Brands as Supply Chain Director, and finally the seventeen-month Future Life cereals contract in Durban that delivered massive bottom-line savings, lifted food-safety standards, installed real-time OEE monitoring and a high-speed packing line that moved the plant from six days a week to four. Closes on the plan to launch his own LinkedIn-fronted Business Transformation Company and the lesson that 'word-of-mouth' contacts and finishing on a high note compound for a lifetime.

# BEVCO, Future Life and Still Going at Seventy-Three

There is a particular silence that arrives the day you cross sixty-five. The retirement notices have been signed, the leaving cards have been read out, the people who promised they would stay in touch have already begun the slow, polite drift back into their own diaries. The garden is suddenly visible from the kitchen window in a way it never quite was before. The phone, for a week or two, sits very still. And the question that arrives in that silence, whether you welcome it or not, is the only question that really matters at that age: are you finished, or are you only between contracts?

I was not finished. I have never, in fact, been particularly good at being finished. Even as a boy on the East Rand, watching my father come up out of the shaft with the dust of Sub Nigel in the creases of his hands, I had the sense that work was not something done to a person, but something a person did with the time they were given. My father did it with a pick and a lamp. I have done it with a clipboard and an OEE screen. The instrument changes; the obligation does not. So when the retirement letters were signed and the cake was eaten and the corporate balloons came down, what I felt was not relief and certainly not nostalgia. What I felt was the quiet, almost shy curiosity of a man who suspects that the most interesting third of his career might still be ahead of him.

It turned out that it was.

## A contract called BEVCO

The first phone call of that third act came from a face I knew very well. The Tiger Brands executive who had originally recruited me into the confectionary group, the man who had handed me the failed two-hundred-million-rand mogul line and watched me put it back into production in six months, had moved on himself. He had been appointed Supply Chain Director of BEVCO, a beverage company in Johannesburg, and he had not forgotten the way I worked. He picked up the phone toward the end of my Tiger Brands contract and asked, in the easy way of a man who already knows the answer, whether I would come and help him with a beverages business.

I have learned, over the years, to take such calls seriously. The single most reliable currency in a long career is not your CV, no matter how carefully you have decorated it. It is the people who have worked next to you in difficult years and would, without hesitation, work next to you again. I had finished on a high note at Tiger Brands. I had finished on a high note at every company before it. That habit, which I had begun almost by accident in my AECI years, was now compounding. Every closing handshake became, quietly, the opening line of the next contract.

BEVCO was a beverage business in the truest sense of the phrase: high volumes, thin margins, ferocious competition, and a customer base that does not forgive a stockout. In the world of pharmaceuticals, where I had spent the long Adcock Ingram years, your moat is regulatory; the product itself is difficult to make and impossible to fake. In the world of confectionary, your moat is brand and distribution. But in beverages, where every cent counts and every shelf is contested, your moat is operating cost. Lose a few percentage points of productivity in a beverage plant and you have not lost an inconvenience. You have lost the business.

I walked the lines as I always did. I have a routine I have followed at every company since SA Oil Mills, and it has never failed me: in the first two weeks I do not write a single email of substance, I do not make a single promise, and I do not propose a single capital project. I walk. I stand on the line next to the operators. I watch a packing machine for an hour without speaking. I count the small stoppages, the micro-changeovers, the inputs that are out of specification, the bottle of cleaning fluid that has been put down in the wrong place because somebody, three months ago, decided it was easier than walking the extra ten metres. I read the maintenance logs and the deviation reports. I have lunch with the shift supervisors. By the end of those two weeks I know what is broken, and almost always I know who knows how to fix it, even if they have stopped telling anyone.

At BEVCO the answer was about productivity. The plant could move, but it could not move the way a modern beverage plant has to move. We attacked the small stoppages first, because in a high-speed line the small stoppages are everything; they look insignificant on any single shift, but compounded over a year they cost you a fortune in lost output. We tightened changeovers, sharpened the maintenance routines, brought the operators into the conversation rather than leaving them outside it. We focused on cost savings via improved productivity, because in a competitive beverage business there is no other option. The man who had recruited me wanted improvements, and over the year I spent there we delivered them.

It was a short contract by the standards of my earlier life. A year, no more. But the BEVCO experience taught me something specific about the new shape of my career, something I had only half-understood at the end of the Tiger Brands stint. The old model, the one I had lived for thirty years, was to join a company, climb inside it, and grow with it. The new model was different. The new model was that companies in trouble needed a transformation engine for twelve months, eighteen months, perhaps two years, and then they needed their own people to run what had been transformed. My role, increasingly, was to leave a stronger company behind me than I had found, and then to leave. That had always been my private definition of leadership. Now it was becoming, more openly, my business.

## Back to Tiger Brands, this time across the country

The phone rang again, and again I knew the voice on the other end. Tiger Brands wanted me back. They had decided that the confectionary group, all of it, needed the same hand that had repaired the mogul line and improved the candy and chocolate operations. This time the role was bigger and the geography was different. I was offered a contract as Supply Chain Director for the confectionary business, with the plants now running across the country, and with the heart of the operation in Durban.

I accepted. I had finished on a high note last time. I had a relationship with the team and with the executives who had hired me. The deciding factor, however, was something quieter than any of those things. It was the conviction, which had only deepened with each contract, that I could now walk into any business, of any size, in any sector, and within two weeks tell the board where the leverage was. I had been training for that capability my whole working life without knowing it: the years on the shaft, the years on the chemical plant, the years in pharmaceuticals, the leather years at Kolosus, the oil mills, the exports, the candies. Each of them had been a different angle on the same underlying machine. The machine is always a machine. The product changes; the operating principles do not.

The new Tiger Brands contract gave me responsibility for the supply chain across all the confectionary plants. Gums and jellies, the line I had already restored. Candy and chocolate. The operations I had transformed before were no longer on probation; they were running, and they had earned their certifications, and the people who ran them were the same people I had stood next to in the early months. There is a particular pleasure in returning to a plant you have transformed and finding that the discipline you put in has held. The 5S markings are still on the floor. The morning huddle is still happening at the start of every shift. The real-time monitoring screen is still on the wall, still telling the truth, still being looked at. The transformation has become the culture, which is the only kind of transformation that actually lasts.

What I had not expected, when I returned to the Durban site, was the welcome. The staff celebrated my return. They jumped up and down and embraced me like I was a member of the family who had been away too long. I have, over a long career, received many gifts from companies. A handshake from a chairman. A bonus letter. A leaving plaque, even one or two awkward speeches. But I have never received a gift quite like that one. It was, very simply, proof that the Trust-and-Inspire philosophy I had built my management style around was not theoretical, was not academic, was not an item from a textbook. It was something the people on the floor had felt, had remembered, and had been willing to demonstrate when I came back.

For the next year I led that supply chain. We protected what had been built. We pushed productivity further. We kept the food safety standards where they needed to be, which in confectionary means above the level of the certifications, not at it. I have always believed that compliance is the minimum bar of a serious business, not the target. The target is to be embarrassed when an auditor finds something you should already have fixed.

And then, before that year was out, Tiger Brands made another request. The Black Cat peanut butter plant in Randfontein had problems, and they wanted me to go and look at it.

## Black Cat and the lesson of the raw material

I have, in the various chapters of this life, transformed many factories. Some of them were misbehaving for management reasons, some for capital reasons, some because a previous executive had bought a plant that did not quite fit the product. Black Cat was different. Black Cat was misbehaving because of the peanut.

Peanut butter is a deceptively complicated product. To a consumer it is a jar of one ingredient, ground and salted, perhaps sweetened. To a manufacturer it is a chemistry experiment that depends on the raw material more than most processors are willing to admit. The peanut comes in graded by size, by oil content, by moisture, by the proportion of broken kernels, by the residual aflatoxin testing, by the season, by the supplier, by the field. Get the wrong peanut and there is no roasting profile in the world that will rescue the product downstream.

When I arrived at the Black Cat plant the line was producing, but it was not producing well. There were processing problems linked, I quickly suspected, to the quality of the raw materials coming in. The people who ran the plant had been treating it as a manufacturing problem. They had been adjusting screws on machines and reviewing the maintenance logs of grinders. I have nothing against adjusting screws on machines, but if the peanut going into the front of the line is variable, no amount of fine-tuning at the back of the line will save you. The first hour I spent in the goods-receiving area told me where most of the answer lay.

I implemented the same toolkit I had brought to every previous Tiger Brands plant. The Real-Time production monitoring system went in, so that the OEE numbers were on the wall and could not be hidden in a monthly report. We put in the same standards, the same housekeeping discipline, the same audit cadence, that the candy and chocolate plants now took for granted. Then I worked with the procurement team and the technical team to attack the raw material problem at its source. We tightened the specifications. We changed the way we tested incoming peanuts. We worked with suppliers, in some cases ruthlessly, to bring consistency to what was arriving at our gates.

The output from the plant increased tremendously. The food safety position was reinforced. The quality certifications, which had been under quiet pressure, were stabilised and improved. Another successful contract period. Another full bonus. Another company left in a better state than I had found it.

It bears noting, because I think it matters, that all of this work, the BEVCO year, the Tiger Brands Supply Chain Director year, the Black Cat year, was done under contract. I had passed the formal retirement age of sixty-five some time before. By the time I closed off the Black Cat assignment I had been working eight years past my retirement age. I was, on the day I packed up my office, seventy-three.

If you are a younger person reading this, especially someone in your fifties wondering whether your best work is behind you, I want you to hear this part very clearly. The single most powerful asset I have at seventy-three is not my CV. It is not even my track record, although that has helped me. It is the dense, quiet network of relationships I built across forty years of work, every one of them watered by the discipline of finishing on a high note. The phone calls that have come to me in this third act have all come from people who knew me in the second. Word of mouth, in a career, is the slowest compounding force there is, and also the most reliable. You are never too old to be successful if you produce quality work. The market will find you, because the people who have worked alongside you will find you for the market.

## The Future Life chapter

The headhunter who called me at the end of the Black Cat contract was talking about a company called Future Life. I knew the brand. Most South African families do. It is a powder cereal business, a porridge brand, that had grown by ten to twenty percent per annum for ten years on the back of a clear nutritional proposition and a loyal consumer base. That kind of growth, sustained over a decade, will outgrow any factory eventually. Future Life had reached the moment in its history where the building it had grown up in could no longer carry the business it had become. The company had moved from its older premises to a new site in Durban, and the new business needed a new operating discipline. The old site, by their own honest assessment, did not comply with food safety standards. Many standards were lacking. They needed someone who had spent a career raising standards, and who could do it without breaking the growth engine in the process.

I relished it.

The seventeen months that followed were, by my own measure, the most successful contract period of the entire third act. Not because the work was harder than what had come before, although it was hard, but because the new Future Life site allowed me to exercise the full range of what I had spent fifty years learning. There is a particular gift in being asked to apply everything at once. The pharmaceutical instincts from Adcock Ingram. The 5S discipline from the Japanese fool-proof philosophy that had become a permanent operating instinct for me. The OEE measurement work from the Tiger Brands confectionary turnarounds. The food-safety standards that had, by then, become as natural to me as breathing. The high-speed packing-line procurement experience from the AECI years. The leadership style I had been refining for four decades.

The first thing I did, as I always do, was walk the plant. I did not promise anything in the first fortnight. I observed. I sat with the operators on the lines. I watched a powder packing machine for an unbroken hour and counted every minor stop. I read the food-safety audit reports and the customer complaint log. I had quiet lunches with the engineering team and the QC team. I let the people who knew the place tell me what they would change if they were given permission to change it. By the end of week two I had a draft of what was needed. By the end of week four I had a plan that the executive team had bought into. By the end of month two we were executing.

The results, when they came, were significant in a way that satisfies an old operating mind. We delivered massive bottom-line savings. Profitability improved tremendously. I installed a real-time production monitoring system, the same approach that had worked at every Tiger Brands plant, which simplified the operation enormously. Once the OEE numbers are visible on a screen at the end of the line, you do not need to argue with anybody about whether a machine is performing. The machine tells you. The operators know it. The supervisors know it. The management knows it. The conversation stops being about blame and starts being about improvement.

The most consequential intervention, however, was the new high-speed packing line. Future Life had been running six days a week to meet demand. Six days, week in and week out, with all the overtime, all the maintenance pressure, and all the labour cost that a six-day operation entails. I specified, procured and installed a new high-speed packing line that lifted output dramatically. The result was that we could now meet demand by working only four days a week. The labour savings were substantial. The maintenance windows opened up. The shift rosters became saner. Productivity improved across the board. Food safety standards were upgraded everywhere. The plant was a different operation from the one I had walked into seventeen months earlier.

I closed that contract at the end of March, on the high note that I have always insisted on for myself. The team celebrated. The executives wrote letters that I have kept. The handover was clean. The systems were running. The standards had been embedded. The OEE screens were on the walls and being watched. The four-day-week pattern was holding. The new packing line was producing. There was nothing left for me to do in that plant that the existing team could not do better and more sustainably than I could.

## Still going at seventy-three

So here I am.

Seventy-three years old. A wife of fifty-one years, who has carried this whole journey with me through every overseas trip and every contract relocation, and who continues to be the steadiest centre of gravity in my life. Four children, all grown. Nine grandchildren. Three great-grandchildren. A long career that, at every reasonable point in the last decade, the world has expected me to wind down. And a phone that, against every demographic prediction, has continued to ring.

The plan from here is not to retire. The plan from here is to formalise the third act.

I am establishing my own Business Transformation Company, fronted on LinkedIn, designed to reach the influential leaders and boards who are quietly wondering whether their operations are as productive, as safe and as profitable as they could be, and who would benefit from a hand that has done this many times before. I have superb testimonials from every assignment of the contract years. I have the BEVCO experience, the second Tiger Brands stint, the Black Cat turnaround, the Future Life transformation, the long pre-contract history before any of those. I have the relationships from forty years of finishing on a high note. I have the body of work to prove that what I do works, and the willingness to put my name in front of the next group of leaders who need a transformation.

It should be mentioned, because it has shaped the work in ways that are not obvious from the outside, that across all of these contracts I have personally carried the responsibility for industrial relations. The annual wage negotiations. The hard conversations about retrenchments where the business required them. The collaborations with shop stewards. The disciplinary hearings that no manager ever enjoys but every senior manager must conduct fairly. That work has built up, year by year, into something I now regard as a separate qualification, an unwritten degree in human relations under pressure. It is not a degree I would wish on a young manager early; the strain is real. But by the third act of a career it is the kind of experience that allows you to walk into any negotiation in the country, on either side of the table, and have a sense of where the room actually is.

I want to underline one more lesson, because it is the one I would most want a younger version of myself to hear. Relationship building is not a soft skill. It is not a sideline to the technical work. It is, in the long arc of a career, the single most important asset you will ever build, and you will only realise this in the years when you have stopped trying to impress anyone. The people you treat well in your thirties are the people who will recruit you in your sixties. The people you take time over in your forties are the people who will write your testimonials in your seventies. Every closing handshake of every contract becomes, in the fullness of time, an opening line. Lean on those relationships when you need them; build them when you do not.

And finish on a high note. Always finish on a high note. Walk out of every company with the standards higher than when you arrived, with the people better trained than when you found them, with the books in better shape than they were on the day you signed the contract, and with a clean office and a courteous goodbye. You may never set foot in that building again. Or, more likely than you think, you may walk back in on a contract ten years later to a team that jumps up and down at your return. Either way, the standard of the leaving is the standard of the legacy.

Who knows what is next.

I do not say that with any anxiety. I say it with the same curiosity I felt as a sixteen-year-old apprentice at Sub Nigel, walking into the first morning of the trade and wondering, quietly and privately, just how far this might go. The instrument has changed many times since then. The clipboard has been an OEE screen, a packing line specification, a leather negotiation in Paris, a chemical plant in Sasolburg, a peanut butter procurement spec, a porridge factory in Durban. The next instrument may very well be a LinkedIn message and a remote first conversation with a board I have not yet met. That is fine with me. The work is the same.

You are never too old to be useful. You are never too old to be needed. You are never too old to take a struggling business by the shoulders and walk it, step by patient step, into the shape it was always meant to be in. The Lord has given me seventy-three years, a faithful wife, a family I am proud of, and a body of experience that, by His grace, still has something to offer.

The cake has long since been eaten, the balloons have long since come down, and the phone, for what it is worth, is still ringing.

I am going to keep answering it.

existingChapters.title: Overseas Exposure: A Global Mindset Built One Trip at a Time
existingChapters.summary: Pulls together the international thread: Germany (Munich, Dusseldorf, Krefeld), Boston, Scotland (East Kilbride, Gleneagles), Italy (Solofra, Capri, Rome), France, Holland, Korea, China, Japan, Malaysia, Singapore, Taiwan, Thailand, Cyprus, Mauritius and across Southern Africa. Argues that overseas exposure resets a leader's benchmark standards, with concrete examples from the Italian Continental Oils commissioning that turned a loss into a profit, the Korean pharmaceutical plant assessment, and a 200% year-on-year Adcock export sales lift.

existingChapters.title: Trust and Inspire: A Management Style for the Modern Workforce
existingChapters.summary: Lays out the core leadership philosophy of the book in full: Trust-and-Inspire over Command-and-Control, the Microsoft Nadella turnaround as proof, emotional intelligence, mentorship, mission-and-vision discipline, the truth that 'structure follows strategy', the cost of poor managers, hiring for attitude over qualifications, celebrating success down to the shopfloor, and the Port Elizabeth banner 'Work Smart and Have Fun'. Includes the Sipho-the-fitter recognition story and the senior manager who thanked him for 'giving them wings to fly'.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Trust and Inspire: A Management Style for the Modern Workforce

There comes a point in a working life when a man stops collecting management techniques and starts trying to name the one thing that has held them all together. I am at that point. I have read most of the books that the airport bookshops have been pressing on executives for forty years, and I have sat through more leadership seminars than I am willing to confess to in print, and I have, on a number of occasions, been the man at the front of the room. None of that, in the end, is what I would put on the first page if I were asked to summarise a half-century of running operations. The first page would have two ideas on it, and the rest of the page would be blank. Trust and Inspire. Not Command and Control.

I want to spend this chapter explaining what I mean by that, because it is the philosophy underneath every turnaround I have ever signed my name to. It is not a slogan I picked up from a course. It was learned, slowly and unfashionably, on chemical plants and pharmaceutical sites and leather floors and confectionery lines, from the people I tried to lead and from the people I watched fail to lead. By the time I sat in my first Managing Director's chair, in Port Elizabeth in my early forties, I already knew what I would not be. The harder work, then and since, has been making sure I am the other thing instead.

## Two styles, and only one of them works

The Command-and-Control style is the easier one to describe, because every one of us has worked under it at some point. It is the style that treats a workforce as a problem of compliance. Instructions come down. Reports go up. The manager's job is to set targets, monitor variances, and bring the hammer when the variances are unfavourable. Staff under that style work with one eye on the spreadsheet and the other on their shoulder. They keep their heads down. They volunteer nothing. They wait to be told.

I have run plants where that was the inherited culture, and I have always recognised it within a week of arriving. The signs are everywhere. The shift supervisor will not speak up in the morning meeting. The operator on the line will let you walk past a problem rather than flag it. The maintenance team will sit on a known defect because the last time they raised one they were blamed for finding it. The whole organisation is, very quietly, choosing not to think. And a plant whose people have stopped thinking is a plant whose output is, sooner or later, going to fall off the back of the truck.

The Trust-and-Inspire style is harder to describe because it looks, to an outsider, like very little is happening. The manager seems to be doing less. The reports are quieter. The instructions are fewer. What is actually happening is that the people closest to the work have been given the dignity of being treated as the experts they very often are. They are trusted with the problem. They are inspired toward a shared goal. They are then permitted, within sensible boundaries, to find their own way to it. The plant, if you have hired and trained the right people, runs better than it ever ran under the man with the megaphone.

I have used a phrase about this with younger managers for many years. Mahatma Gandhi, in one of his less-quoted observations, said that human beings only use a small percentage of what they are capable of. He was making a point about humanity in general, but it has always read to me as a point about management. The secret, in any organisation, lies in extracting people's capability, and you do not extract it by shouting at them. You extract it by trusting them and by giving them something worth being inspired by. The leader who cannot do those two things is, in my honest experience, leaving most of his workforce's value on the table.

## The proof from Redmond

I have always been wary, when I make this argument in a boardroom, of sounding like a man who is preaching philosophy at people who have a P&L to defend. So I have, for some years now, kept a single contemporary example in my pocket for those conversations. It is the example of Microsoft.

When Satya Nadella took over the company in 2014, the market valued Microsoft at about three hundred billion dollars. By any reasonable measure it was already a successful business. The problem was that, by every internal measure that mattered, it had stopped behaving like one. The internal culture had become famously combative. Teams hoarded information from each other. Managers measured each other on stack rankings that punished collaboration. The company that had once defined the personal computer industry was, behind the glass doors, eating itself.

Nadella's intervention was not, in the first instance, technical. He did not announce a new operating system or a reorganisation of the product groups. He announced, in language that was uncomfortable for many of the old hands, that the company was going to operate from a different mindset. Curiosity over knowing. Learning over performing. Empathy as a leadership value. Trust as the default. By the time I write this, Microsoft's market value sits somewhere above two trillion dollars. Whatever you think of the man and the company, those are not numbers that argue for the Command-and-Control playbook. They are numbers that argue for what happens to a company when the leader at the top trusts the people underneath him to do their best work, and inspires them toward a vision they can see themselves in.

I do not raise the Microsoft story because I think small South African operations should imitate Silicon Valley. I raise it because it puts to bed, in the most public possible way, the lazy argument that Trust-and-Inspire is a soft style for a soft economy. It is not. It is the operating model of the highest-performing technology company in the world. If it is good enough for two trillion dollars in market value, it is good enough for the confectionery plant in Durban or the porridge factory in Pinetown.

## Emotional intelligence, and the kind of mentor you should have

Trust-and-Inspire is not a personality trait, and it is not a slogan you can hang in the reception area. It is built on top of two more practical skills, and a manager who lacks them cannot fake it for long.

The first is emotional intelligence. I am not, by temperament, a man who was raised on that phrase. I was raised on the East Rand by a churchgoing father and an industrious mother, and they had a different vocabulary for what is now called EQ. They called it being decent. They called it knowing how to read a room. They called it not embarrassing people. Whatever the language, the underlying skill is the same. It is the ability to notice what is going on inside yourself and inside the other person, in real time, and to manage your behaviour in light of it. A manager who cannot do that will, sooner or later, blow up a relationship that took years to build, and he will not even know which sentence did it.

I have come to believe that emotional intelligence is the single most under-discussed competence in modern engineering and operations management. Most of the technical people I have trained alongside, my younger self included, were taught very thoroughly how a process runs and very poorly how a human being runs. The result is a generation of competent engineers who become incompetent managers the day they are first asked to lead a team. People will forget what you have said. People will forget what you have done. But they will never forget how you made them feel. That sentence, which I picked up from somewhere a long time ago and have never been able to improve on, is the entire syllabus of management in one line.

The second practical skill is the willingness to use, and to be, a mentor. I have had mentors all my life, and I have tried, with mixed success, to be one to others. The criterion that has mattered most to me, when I have chosen the mentors I trust, is integrity. A mentor must be someone who will tell you the truth, even when the truth is awkward, and who you would in turn be willing to tell the truth to. A network of high-integrity colleagues like that, built up patiently over decades, is the single most valuable professional asset I have. It is more valuable than my CV. It is more valuable than any award I have received. The phone calls that have come to me in this third act of my career have all come from inside that network, and most of those people I first earned the trust of in the 1980s.

If you are young as you read this, find one or two of those people early. Not flatterers, not seniors who you hope will sponsor you, but peers and superiors who you can trust to push back when you are wrong and who you will, in your turn, push back on. Then keep them, all your working life. They are the cheapest insurance policy a career has on offer.

## Mission, vision and the disciplined plan

There is a particular failure of management that I have walked into more often than I expected, in the second half of my career, and it is a failure of strategy rather than execution. I have arrived at companies, more than once, that had no documented strategy at all for the function I was being asked to take over. The annual budget would be carefully prepared, with line-item discipline that would have impressed an actuary, but the budget would not be anchored to any actual strategic plan. There would be no clear answer to the question of where the business was trying to go in three years' time, or why, or how. The mission statement, where one existed, would be a paragraph of generic phrases that nobody on the shopfloor could have repeated under oath.

I have, in every business I have led, insisted on something different. Before the budget is signed off, there must be a strategy. Before the strategy is signed off, there must be a clear mission and a clear vision that the workforce can repeat in their own words. The mission tells you what you are for. The vision tells you what you are aiming at. The strategy tells you how you intend to get there. The budget then funds the strategy. In that order. Not the other way around. Companies that build their budgets first and look around for a strategy afterwards are companies that, in my experience, will eventually drift into a slow operational decline that nobody can quite explain.

There is one further point on this, and it is one I have repeated in many boardrooms because it is regularly forgotten. **Structure follows strategy.** When the strategy of a business changes, the structure has to change with it. The reporting lines, the functional boundaries, the spans of control, the way roles are described — all of those have to be redrawn to support the new strategic direction. Many companies do not do this. They announce a new strategy on the first slide of the year, and then they try to deliver it through the same organogram that supported last year's strategy. Implementation, when this happens, becomes a problem nobody can pin down. It is not a problem of people. It is a problem of architecture.

## The cost of a poor manager

I have spent so much of this chapter on what good management looks like that I should be honest, too, about what poor management costs. Across my career, when I have walked into a part of a business that was underperforming, I have looked first at the people. Without exception, when the staff were the problem, the underlying cause was the manager. The wrong person at the top had been hired, or retained, or promoted, and the cascade of disengagement underneath that person was a perfectly predictable consequence.

People do not leave bad jobs. They leave bad bosses. I have seen good operators resign within weeks of a new shift manager being appointed who treated them poorly. I have seen technical brilliance disappear from a department within a year of a senior leader being installed who did not respect the function. Bad managers are bad for business, and they are even worse for their employees, and any senior leader who does not move quickly to address poor management in their organisation is, in effect, choosing to subsidise it.

The other awkward truth I have observed, particularly later in my career, is the asymmetry between the floor and the office. The shopfloor staff in a factory often stay for twenty years, raising their families on that pay cheque, while the management above them changes every two or three. Each new manager arrives with new ideas, holds a few impressive meetings, sometimes commissions a few impressive consultants, and is then gone within twenty-four months. The new ideas disappear with them. The next manager arrives and starts the cycle again. The shopfloor staff, who have been watching this for two decades, are too polite to laugh out loud, but they have stopped taking any of it seriously. Only a small number of senior leaders can build something with their people that lasts beyond their own tenure. Those are the leaders who tend, in my experience, to lead through Trust-and-Inspire rather than through edict.

## Hire for attitude

I will say something here that I have said many times in interviews and in mentoring sessions, and which still sometimes raises an eyebrow in an HR department. Where two candidates are close, hire for attitude over qualifications. I do not mean that qualifications do not matter. Of course they do. I am writing this as a man who studied at night for years to earn the diplomas that opened my doors. But beyond a sensible threshold of competence, the deciding variable is attitude, every time. The candidate with the better paperwork and the wrong attitude will cost you more than the candidate with the lesser paperwork and the right one. The wrong attitude takes up a disproportionate share of your management time, and worse than that, it lowers the standard of every team it joins.

The right attitude, by contrast, is teachable but not transferable. You can teach a person with the right attitude almost any technical skill your business needs. You cannot teach a person with the wrong attitude to care. The market has known this for a very long time. *Ability is what you are capable of doing; motivation determines what you do; attitude determines how well you do it.* That sentence has hung in my head for most of my management years, and it has saved me from several hires I might otherwise have made.

## Celebrating success, all the way to the floor

Trust-and-Inspire is not complete without one further habit, and it is the one most often left out of the management courses I have attended. You must celebrate success, and you must celebrate it down to the shopfloor.

In too many companies the celebration of a good year stops at the management level. The directors get the bonus. The senior managers get the certificate. The floor staff, who actually moved the boxes and ran the machines and made the product that delivered the result, get a memo. That asymmetry is felt. It is not a small thing. People at every level of an operation want to know that what they did mattered, and they want to know it in a form they can take home and show to their families.

I have made it my private rule, at every site I have run, to recognise good work at the level it actually happens. I have walked the floor at the end of a successful project and shaken the hands of the operators who delivered it. I have asked supervisors to put up the names of the shift teams who hit a difficult target that week. I have personally invited artisans into management meetings, not to present, but to be thanked. None of those gestures cost the business anything meaningful. All of them changed, in small but cumulative ways, what people felt about coming to work the next morning.

## The banner in Port Elizabeth

When I took over as Managing Director in Port Elizabeth, I had a banner put up at the entrance of the company. It was not subtle. It read, in large clear letters that you could not miss as you walked through the gate, **WORK SMART AND HAVE FUN**.

I meant every word of it, and I made sure the company meant it too. Work Smart was not a soft phrase. It was an instruction. It meant that effort without direction was not what we were paid for. It meant that we were going to plan our work, sequence it properly, automate what should be automated, and stop confusing motion with progress. It meant we were going to respect each other's time. It meant we were going to deliver against clear targets and measure ourselves honestly against them.

Have Fun was the other half of the instruction, and it was just as important. A workplace where people are afraid to laugh, afraid to put up a photograph of their children on the noticeboard, afraid to be themselves between the morning briefing and the end of shift, is a workplace that has quietly stopped being a place where excellent work is possible. I wanted my plant to be a place where people enjoyed their work as much as they were proud of it. We held social events. We celebrated milestones. We made room for the small humanities of a working week. The banner was, in its way, a contract between me and the workforce: we will work hard and intelligently, and we will treat each other like the human beings we are.

## Sipho, the artisan fitter

I want to tell one story before I close this chapter, because no philosophy of management is worth more than the people it produces. The story is from my Adcock Ingram years, in the middle of a supply chain overhaul. We had been struggling for some weeks with downtime on one of our critical production lines. A persistent maintenance fault had become a thorn in the side of the operations team, eating into output and into morale. The leadership group was, at the time, consumed with the broader turnaround plan. The fault on that one line was, in the cold balance of priorities, a problem we had not yet given proper attention to.

It was Sipho who solved it. He was a soft-spoken artisan fitter, and he took it upon himself, in his own time and without being asked, to diagnose the root cause. He went through the manuals. He consulted retired colleagues whose names he had kept from earlier projects. Within a few days he came forward with a small, elegant redesign of the sealing system that eliminated the fault entirely. The line returned to full productivity. There had been no fanfare. There had been no memo. He had simply taken ownership of a problem the rest of us had not yet reached, and he had fixed it.

When I found out what he had done, I invited him to the next management meeting. He was not invited to report. He was invited to be recognised. I told the broader team his story in front of him, in plain language. I described his ownership, his curiosity, his willingness to use his own evenings to dig into something nobody had asked him to dig into. We presented him with a framed certificate, which was the easy part. But more importantly, in that room, we gave him a voice. He walked out of that meeting a different man, and I have always believed that a future leader was born in our company that afternoon.

That moment taught me, more clearly than any course I have ever attended, what recognition is actually for. Recognition is not about ceremony. It is about dignity. Celebration is not a distraction from work. It is what gives work its meaning. The framed certificate cost us almost nothing. The dignity it carried changed how Sipho saw his place in the company, and how every colleague of his at that meeting understood what we were prepared to notice.

## Wings to fly

I will end this chapter with the words of a senior manager rather than my own. When I left Tiger Brands at the end of my contract there, he came to find me in the corridor on my last day. He shook my hand. He thanked me for what he called my delegating style. And he said something I have never forgotten. He said I had given them wings to fly.

I have, in the years since, often turned that phrase over in my head. I do not think he was being sentimental. I think he was reporting, honestly, what the Trust-and-Inspire style had produced inside his own working life. He had been trusted with real authority. He had been inspired by a vision he could see himself in. He had been given the space to grow into a manager he might not have become under a different leader. By the time I walked out of the building he was a more capable, more confident, more useful executive than he had been when I arrived. None of that growth had cost the company anything. All of it would compound, in his career, long after my own name had faded from the staff lists.

That, in the end, is what this style is for. Not for the manager who practises it. Not for the leader whose picture might one day go on a wall. It is for the people on the other side of the table, who deserve to be trusted with the work they were hired to do, and who, when they are, will rise further than any of us, including themselves, would have predicted on the day they were first interviewed.

If I am remembered for one thing in this long career, I would like it to be that. Not the turnarounds, not the contracts, not the OEE screens, not the four-day weeks, not the export growth or the safety records. Just the small, quiet sentence in a corridor at Tiger Brands, from a man who is now leading his own team somewhere in this country, saying that he was once given wings to fly.

That, more than any market valuation, is what a management style is worth.

existingChapters.title: Sport, Discipline and the Hidden Curriculum of the Boardroom
existingChapters.summary: Tells the full sporting story: school rugby, athletics and gymnastics, the silver medal at a training camp, becoming a top trampolinist, the pivot to tennis through his sister and brother-in-law, four Vaal Triangle titles, twenty career tournament wins, coaching the AECI Dusseldorf staff for two months, beating the Free State number one and South African under-16 number one Pieter Aldrich. Covers the 3-handicap golf, the Macau Vlei winning 71 gross, the hole-in-one at Vereeniging Country Club, Gleneagles, and the audacious 1987 Alberton tennis-and-squash double crown won from forty-fourth on the squash ladder.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Sport, Discipline and the Hidden Curriculum of the Boardroom

There is a part of my story that some readers might be tempted to skip — the chapter where the engineer puts down the spanner and picks up the racquet. Please do not skip it. Of all the threads that run through my life, none has taught me more about leadership, more about discipline, and more about how a person from a mine house on the East Rand can stand his ground in any room in the world than the years I spent training, competing and losing on the sports field. Sport was not a hobby alongside my career. Sport was the laboratory in which I learned the habits that would later let me transform companies.

I am seventy-three as I write this. I still go to the gym on a regular basis, I still watch every Springbok Test that is on television, and I still feel the same quiet pleasure when I see a perfectly struck cover drive, a flat second serve that catches the line, or a putt that drops from twenty feet. None of what I am about to tell you in this chapter would have been possible without good health, and good health, in turn, has been the by-product of a half-century of training and a steady diet. The body and the mind are not two separate accounts. They are one balance sheet, and you cannot run a deficit on the physical side and expect your career to keep paying dividends.

## The school years — rugby, athletics and a silver medal at gymnastics

Like most South African boys of my generation, I started with rugby. I played at school and I enjoyed it for what it was — a game that taught you to tackle a boy bigger than yourself, to get up off the ground when the next ruck was already forming, and to put the team's result above your own bruises. I was never going to play Currie Cup rugby, and I knew it early, but the lessons of the scrum and the tackle are with me still. There is a particular kind of South African discipline that comes from a winter afternoon on a hard mid-Reef field, with your jersey stuck to your back with sweat and dust, and a coach telling you that the next minute is the only one that matters. I think most of the men of my generation who went on to run businesses learned something about persistence from rugby long before they ever read a management book.

Athletics came naturally after that. I was not the fastest boy at the school, but I was competitive, and competitive at school athletics teaches you a useful lesson — that the boy who looks faster in the warm-up is not always the boy who breaks the tape. I learned to keep something back, to read the race, and to push when others were tightening. It is the same lesson I would use, three decades later, when I had to sequence a R200-million plant turnaround at Tiger Brands. The first lap is not the lap that matters. The last one is.

Gymnastics was the surprise. At a training camp during high school I came away with a silver medal — a small piece of metal that no one outside our family has ever seen, but a piece of metal that taught me what coordinated, repeated, full-body discipline feels like. From gymnastics I drifted naturally into trampolining, and there I had real ability. A trampolinist learns three things very quickly. The first is that you cannot fake a routine — every flaw is visible in the air. The second is that confidence and fear sit on either side of the same coin, and you decide every morning which side faces up. The third is that the people who make it look effortless are the people who have done it ten thousand times in private. Twenty-five years later, when a German foreman in Dusseldorf told me my plant-repair schedule was impossible, the trampolinist in me was the one who answered: I have been doing the equivalent of this routine for two decades.

## The pivot to tennis — and the four Vaal Triangle titles

The sport that truly took me, though, was tennis. It came into my life sideways, the way the best things do. My sister had married a man who loved the game, and the two of them invited me into it almost casually. I did not know at the time that I had just walked through the doorway of the sport that would shape the rest of my life.

Tennis is a deceptively complete teacher. It looks like a simple, individual game — two players, a strip of fenced concrete or grass, a ball, a net — but it is one of the most demanding management exercises ever invented. You are the strategist, the tactician, the player, the coach and the psychologist, all at once, with no time-outs and no team-mates to pass to. Every point is a complete cycle of plan, execute, observe and adjust. Every match is a project. Every season is a business plan. I sometimes joke with younger managers that if you can win a third-set tie-break from 4-6 down on your opponent's serve, you can chair almost any executive meeting that the world is ever going to throw at you.

I won, by my honest count, about twenty titles over the course of my career. Some were club titles, some were regional, some were invitational tournaments arranged through the network of clubs across the Reef, the Vaal and the Eastern Cape. The four I remember best are the four Vaal Triangle championships I won in succession — a provincial-level tournament with a deep field and a long memory. The first one is the one you remember as the breakthrough; the fourth one is the one you remember as a habit. Anyone who has won the same competition four times will tell you the same thing — by the fourth one you are no longer playing the other players, you are playing your own standards. The opponent across the net is a circumstance. The opponent in your own head is the contest.

## A tennis court at home, and sixty pupils a week

When my career began to take me higher into AECI and then into Adcock Ingram, we built a tennis court at our home. That court was, in some ways, the most important capital expenditure of my life. It was where my children learned the game. It was where I coached, on most weeks, around sixty pupils. And it was where I held myself accountable when the working week had ended and I still had a forehand to sharpen.

At one stage I had under my coaching the champions in every junior age band the club ran — the under-12, under-14, under-16 and under-18 boys' champions, all coming through my court. Coaching is the part of sport that prepares you most directly for management. When a fifteen-year-old hits a forehand into the net for the fourth time in a row, you do not shout at him. You do not threaten to drop him from the squad. You walk over, you change one variable — the grip, the foot position, the toss height, the contact point — and you ask him to try again. You congratulate him on the part that was right, and you correct, gently, the part that was wrong. It is exactly what good leaders do with engineers and operators on a factory floor, and a coach who has done it for ten years has the muscle memory long before the leadership book has been written.

There is another part of coaching that I think very few managers fully grasp until they have done it themselves. You cannot, as a coach, win the match for your player. Your job is to give them a structure, a confidence, a set of options and a state of mind, and then to sit down and let them play. The temptation in business is to take the racquet out of the subordinate's hand and play the point yourself. It is always the wrong instinct. The court — like the factory — belongs to the people who are actually on it.

## Coaching the AECI Germans for two months in Dusseldorf

The second-most unlikely tennis assignment of my life — the most unlikely being the Alberton story I will come to in a moment — happened during my AECI assignment in Dusseldorf. I had been sent across to specify and supervise a reactor repair, and somewhere between the technical meetings and the night shifts I had agreed to mention, casually, that I played tennis. The German colleagues seized on it. Within a week I was on the AECI European staff's club courts every available evening, and within a fortnight I had been asked to coach the full company contingent for two months.

It was an extraordinary experience for a young South African engineer. The Germans took to coaching the way they take to most things — with seriousness, punctuality and an absolute respect for the instructor while the lesson was running. By the time I left, the standard of play across the group had risen visibly, and the relationships I had built around the net carried directly into the technical meetings and the nightshift schedules. The reactor repair came in thirty days early. I have never been able to disentangle the influence of the tennis court from the success of that contract. I do not think the two can be separated.

## Beating the Free State number one and Pieter Aldrich

If you play first-league tennis for long enough in South Africa, you will eventually meet players whose names you later read on international result sheets. I was lucky enough to do so twice in a way that has stayed with me. The first was in a provincial match against the Free State, when I drew their number one player in the singles. He was a player of real ability and I was, on paper, the underdog. The match went the distance, but I won it, and I won it because I had played him in my head a dozen times before I walked on court. I had planned the first three points of his service game and the first three points of mine. He was a better striker of the ball than I was. He was not, on that afternoon, a better planner.

The second match is the one I most often tell young players about, because the player on the other side of the net was Pieter Aldrich. He was at that stage the South African under-16 number one, and would later become one of the top ten men's doubles players in the world. I will not pretend that beating him at that stage of his career was beating the player he would become. But beating him on the day we played taught me that you do not concede a match because the name on the draw sheet has international ambitions. You concede a match by deciding, before you walk on, that the other person is going to win. That decision is yours alone, and if you refuse to make it, anything is possible.

## Late twenties, golf, and a hole-in-one at Vereeniging

In my late twenties I added golf to the rotation. I had no intention, when I started, of becoming a serious golfer. I was a tennis player who needed a Sunday morning game with friends. But the discipline that had taken me through tennis transferred. Within about two years I had played myself down to a three-handicap, which in club golf is a respectable place to live.
