My best tournament round was a 71 gross at the Macau Vlei Golf Course in Vereeniging, off an eight-handicap. I won the competition that day. I remember the round in some detail. I remember the par fives I had decided, before the first tee, that I would play conservatively. I remember the par threes I had decided I would attack. I remember three putts that dropped on the front nine and gave me the confidence to play the back nine without thinking too hard. A 71 gross at Macau Vlei is not a Major championship, but it was my Major, and the discipline that produced it was the same discipline that, a few months later, would let me sit in a Tiger Brands boardroom and recommend a R10-million automation capex with full conviction.

I had a hole-in-one at the Vereeniging Country Club on a par three I had played dozens of times. Anyone who has ever had a hole-in-one will tell you the same thing: you do not remember the ball going in. You remember the silence as it tracked towards the flag, and the cheer half a second after it dropped. It is the closest thing in golf to the moment, in business, when a strategy you have been working on for six months finally lands and you see the operators on the factory floor celebrate without being asked.

The other course I will mention because it has a place in my memory is Gleneagles in Scotland. I was fortunate enough, during a business trip, to play eighteen holes on the King's Course at Gleneagles — the same complex where the 2014 Ryder Cup was contested. To stand on the first tee at Gleneagles as a boy from Sub Nigel mine housing is a kind of full-stop. I am not a sentimental man, but I allow myself a moment of quiet wonder about that day every now and again. The boy who walked behind his father to church on the East Rand had no idea that the same legs would one day walk a Ryder Cup course in Perthshire.

## The 1987 Alberton double — won from forty-fourth on the squash ladder

Now we come to the story that I tell young people more than any other, because it is the cleanest example I know of the principle that runs through every chapter of this book: if you put your mind to something — even the apparently impossible — it can be achieved.

By my late twenties I was, in addition to my tennis, playing squash. The two games are cousins, and a good tennis player who is willing to put in the conditioning work can develop into a good squash player surprisingly quickly. I enjoyed the speed of the game and the associated fitness. At that stage I was the Alberton tennis champion — I had been for a few years — and I was beginning to climb the squash ladder at the same club.

One night in the early part of 1987, after a game of squash, the senior players were standing in the bar discussing who was likely to be the squash champion later that year. I do not know exactly what came over me. Perhaps it was the residual confidence from another tennis title, perhaps it was the adrenaline of a game that had gone the distance, perhaps it was the look of mild dismissal on the face of one of the top-ranked squash players. Whatever it was, I threw my hat in the mix. I said, in front of every senior player at the club, that I would win both the Alberton Tennis and the Alberton Squash titles that year.

The room laughed. They were entitled to. At that stage I was number forty-four on the squash ladder at the club. Forty-four. There were forty-three players ahead of me in the queue for that crown, including several who had been club champions and several who would have been favoured by any reasonable bookmaker. The prediction was, on paper, absurd.

The prediction was also a contract I had signed with myself in front of witnesses, which is the most binding kind of contract there is. I went home that night and I made a plan.

I started doing what we used to call roadwork — jogging and sprinting almost every morning in the months between that conversation and the championships in September. I built my fitness with the squash season specifically in mind. Squash demands an aerobic and an anaerobic engine simultaneously, and I knew that if I could outlast my opponents, I could compensate for the technical gap that would still exist between me and the top of the ladder.

I trained until I could run five kilometres in nineteen minutes and thirty seconds. I am still proud of that time. I was in my late twenties, holding down a senior position at Adcock Ingram, raising young children with Antonette, and running a five-kilometre time that any club athlete would respect. The fitness was the foundation. The squash technique I sharpened in parallel, climbing the club ladder one match at a time as the year wore on. From forty-four, I worked my way into the top ten.

The Tennis championship at Alberton came first. I won it comfortably. I was so fit from the squash preparation that I was almost playing a different sport. My opponents were playing tennis. I was playing endurance with a racquet in my hand. The trophy went on the shelf, and the squash championship was a few weeks away.

The squash championship was, as everyone in the club had expected, harder. The technical gap between number forty-four and the top of the ladder does not entirely close in a single year of training. But by then I was inside the top ten, my fitness was as good as anyone's in the draw, and I had spent every spare evening on the squash court. I won my early-round matches. I won my quarter-final and my semi-final. And then I won the final, and I was crowned the new Alberton squash champion.

The same year. The same club. Both titles. From forty-fourth on the squash ladder.

I do not tell that story to brag. I am too old for bragging, and Antonette would not allow it in any case. I tell it because it is the cleanest evidence I have ever experienced in my own life of a principle that has run through every job I have ever held: you can achieve almost anything you put your mind to, provided you are willing to do the unglamorous, repeated, daily work that no one else is watching.

## A Saturday in 1987

Some weeks during that 1987 build-up, my Saturday looked like this. Squash from six to seven in the morning, before most of Alberton was awake. Eighteen holes of golf from eight until one, walking the course in a four-ball. First-league tennis from two in the afternoon, often running into early evening. Then home, a meal cooked by Antonette, and bed by ten o'clock so that I could be at the AECI or Adcock Ingram office on Monday morning at full capacity.

That Saturday schedule is not a recommendation for everyone. It worked because I had a wife who understood what I was building, children who were old enough by then to come with me to several of the events, a strong constitution, and an instinct for sequencing. But it makes the point that the chapters of a life are not lived in series. They are lived in parallel. The discipline that produced a 71 at Macau Vlei was the same discipline that produced the 50 per cent output lift on the AECI cyanide plant. The fitness that produced the squash title was the same fitness that allowed me, ten years later, to fly to Munich, to Boston, to Solofra and to Seoul in a single quarter without losing my footing on any of them.

## The hidden curriculum

When people ask me what business school I went to, I list, honestly, the qualifications I hold — the Mechanical Engineering Diploma, the diploma in Company Direction, the final-year subjects of the International MBA, the intensive MBA short course at the University of Cape Town. But the deeper education was the one nobody examined me on. It was the curriculum hidden inside three decades of sport.

The first lesson the curriculum teaches you is to **plan beyond the visible scoreboard.** A tennis player who only watches the score is a player who reacts. A tennis player who watches patterns — which serve has produced the most cheap points, which side of the court the opponent's backhand is leaking, which break point came after which rally length — is a player who controls. In business, the manager who only watches the monthly P&L is reacting. The manager who watches the leading indicators — the OEE numbers, the staff turnover, the waste percentages, the customer complaint trend — is controlling.

The second lesson is to **train for the opponent you have not yet met.** When I trained for the Alberton squash championship, I trained for an opponent better than any of the players I had seen on the ladder that year. The opponent who finally faced me in the final was, in fact, the easier opponent of the two I had prepared for. When I walked into Kolosus Holdings in Port Elizabeth, I had already, in my head, prepared for a competitive environment more brutal than the one I actually found. The Italian leather buyers who arrived in our offices were tough negotiators. They were not the toughest negotiators I had rehearsed against in my head.

The third lesson is to **lose well.** Anyone who has played sport seriously has lost more matches than they have won. The way you carry a loss is the single most important thing about you, because the next match always begins inside the loss of the previous one. When a R200-million Tiger Brands gum-and-jelly installation went wrong before I arrived, the staff were carrying the loss badly. Half my work was simply to teach them how to set the loss down — how to look at it, understand it, learn from it, and walk on to the next mogul line repair without dragging the failure behind them.

The fourth lesson is to **respect your opponent without fearing them.** When I drew the Free State number one, I respected him. I did not fear him. There is a profound difference. Respect makes you prepare. Fear makes you concede. When I walked into the AECI specification meeting in Dusseldorf with German contractors who had built more reactors than I had ever seen, I respected them. I did not fear them. Respect made me ask better questions. Fear would have made me accept their first answer.

The fifth lesson is to **show up early.** A tennis player who is on court a quarter of an hour before the warm-up has already won something the other player has not. A golfer who has played the front nine in his head before he walks to the first tee has already shaped the round. A businessman who has read the board pack on Friday afternoon, for the meeting on Monday morning, is operating in a different time zone from his colleagues. Sport teaches you that the work that wins the contest is the work nobody can see at the time of the contest.

## Sport and the people I love

I would be writing dishonestly if I left this chapter without acknowledging the cost. The Saturdays I have described — squash, golf, tennis — were Saturdays during which my wife was at home with our children, often without me, doing the quiet, undramatic work of building a Christ-centred family. Sport gave me discipline. Antonette gave the children stability. The two cannot be separated, and I owe her, in this chapter as in every other, the debt that no amount of trophies could repay.

I have also been blessed to see sport pass into the next generation. My daughters and sons won tennis tournaments of their own. My youngest son, Anton, was chosen to play squash for South Africa and became a prefect at his high school. To see the children of an apprentice Boilermaker line up to represent their country in the same sports that had carried their father is the kind of moment that sits, undeclared, on a man's mental list of unique life moments. It sits there alongside the day Mr Mandela accepted a cheque for his seventy-fifth birthday celebrations in Port Elizabeth, the day I attended the 1995 Rugby World Cup final at Ellis Park with my management team as managing director, and the day I stood in the centre of the field at Murrayfield in Scotland. The list is not a brag list. It is a list of moments that would have astonished the sixteen-year-old apprentice who was riding the cage down at Sub Nigel on his first morning underground.

## Still going at seventy-three

I am still in the gym, still watching every Test that matters, still recommending to every young manager I mentor that they pick a sport, any sport, and stay with it long enough to learn what it is trying to teach them. I do not believe you can run a complex factory if you cannot run yourself. I do not believe you can transform a business if you cannot transform your own habits. And I do not believe you can lead a team of fifteen hundred people, the way I did at Tiger Brands, if you have never been the captain of even a club tennis side and felt the weight of fourteen other players watching your second serve at five-all.

If you take one thing from this chapter, take this. The boardroom does not have a hidden curriculum. The hidden curriculum is the same one your school sports master tried to teach you when you were thirteen — show up, train when no one is watching, plan your point before you play it, respect your opponent, lose well, and never let the ladder you are currently on tell you what ladder you are allowed to climb to. Forty-fourth on the squash ladder one January. Champion the same September. That is not luck. That is the curriculum.

And that curriculum, more than any qualification on my wall, is what carried me from a miner's son on the East Rand to the managing director's office, and on, eventually, to the Business Transformation contracts that I am still being asked to lead today at seventy-three.

It is the same curriculum that is available, free of charge, to every reader of this book. All you have to do is sign the contract with yourself, in front of witnesses if possible, and then go and do the unglamorous, repeated, daily work that no one else can see.

The ladder will take care of itself.

existingChapters.title: Faith, Family and the AI Age: Life Learnings and What Comes Next
existingChapters.summary: Closes the book by braiding the three remaining threads: a 51-year marriage to Antonette, four children, nine grandchildren and three great-grandchildren as the foundation under everything; the explicit Christian faith and the bible verses that anchor key decisions; and the leadership challenge of the AI age, where the leaders who use AI will replace those who don't, but where empathy, integrity and the human touch remain non-negotiable. Distills the Key Focus Areas and Life Learnings, the unique-life-moments list, the Mandela cheque, the 1995 Rugby World Cup, and the testimonials into a final summary of principles for the next generation.
existingChapters.contentMarkdown:
# Faith, Family and the AI Age: Life Learnings and What Comes Next

There is a temptation, when a man sits down to write the last chapter of a book about his working life, to make the closing pages sound grander than the man himself. I am not going to do that. I am seventy-three. I have been married to Antonette for fifty-one years. We have four children, nine grandchildren and three great-grandchildren. I still read my bible most mornings, I still go to the gym on a regular basis, and I still answer the phone when a business that needs transforming asks whether I have the time. Those four sentences, taken together, are the truest summary I can give of what has actually mattered. Everything else in this book has been the scaffolding underneath them.

I want to use this final chapter to braid the three threads that have run, mostly quietly, behind the trade tests and the turnarounds and the leather fairs in Paris. The first is the family that has been the foundation of every contract I have ever signed. The second is the explicit Christian faith that has anchored the decisions no boardroom could decide for me. The third is the leadership question that is sitting on my desk this year, and on yours, whether you are forty or sixty or twenty-two — what does it mean to lead in the age of artificial intelligence, when the technology in front of us is shifting faster than any new machine I ever commissioned and the people behind us still need exactly the same thing they have always needed, which is to be seen, trusted and inspired.

I will close, as I have closed every chapter of my career, with a high note and a clean office.

## Antonette: the foundation under everything

I have written, in earlier chapters, about contracts and capex and exports. I have written about the German foreman in Dusseldorf, the leather fairs in Paris, the candy lines in East London, the peanut butter raw-material problem at Black Cat, and the seventy-three-year-old who is still being asked to lead Business Transformation contracts. None of those pages would have an author behind them if not for one woman.

Antonette and I have been married for fifty-one years. That is not a number I write lightly. Fifty-one years is more than two-thirds of my life. It is older than democratic South Africa. It is older than the company that first sent me to Germany, older than the apprentice who became a Boilermaker, and older than every job title that ever appeared on a business card with my name on it. The marriage came first chronologically and it has come first in every important sense ever since.

I have said in earlier chapters that on the nights I was home, I was home — briefcase in the study, phone on silent, eyes at the dinner table. I would like to expand that, for the younger reader, into something a little more practical. A long marriage, especially a marriage stretched across the contract life I lived, is not held together by anniversaries. It is held together by a thousand small, undramatic decisions taken in the middle of ordinary weeks, when no one is watching, and almost all of them are decisions to put a person ahead of a problem. The packing line will be there in the morning. Antonette will be there in the morning too — but only if you have, in the years before that morning, treated her as the senior partner she actually is.

We raised four children together. Each of them has, in their own way, surprised me — and you have already met my youngest in passing in the earlier sport chapter, my son Anton, who was chosen to play squash for South Africa and became a prefect at his high school. The other three have built their own lives, each with the same quiet, undramatic work ethic that I would like to think Antonette and I tried to model. The arithmetic now stands at nine grandchildren and three great-grandchildren, and the family WhatsApp group on a Sunday evening produces a level of noise and good news that, frankly, makes a board call feel quiet by comparison.

I want to say something here that I have not, perhaps, said often enough in print or in person. The career you have read about in this book is, in any honest accounting, half hers. The years I was away in Port Elizabeth while two of our grown children stayed behind in Johannesburg; the years I was driving back and forth to East London for the confectionery contracts; the early mornings before flights to Munich and Boston and Solofra and Seoul; the late evenings at OEE screens in Tiger Brands — Antonette held the home steady through every one of them, without complaint, and without ever letting the children feel that their father had stepped back. Whatever transformation work I have been able to do in the boardroom is, in its quieter dimension, a transformation she made possible in the living room. The debt is not one I can repay in this lifetime. I can only acknowledge it, here, in the part of the book that is going to outlive both of us.

## The faith that anchored every decision

I grew up in a mine house on the East Rand, the youngest of five, with a devout Christian father who spent his life underground at Sub Nigel Gold Mine and a shy, industrious mother who held the home together on a miner's wage. Sunday church was not a debate in our home; it was the rhythm of the week. The bible was on the side table, and the side table was where the side table belonged. I have, by the grace of God, never lost that rhythm.

I am not, and have never been, a man who quotes scripture in a meeting to win an argument. I do not believe that is what scripture is for. But I will tell you, candidly, that the difficult decisions of my career — the retrenchment letters I had to sign at SA Oil Mills, the closure decisions at Kolosus, the wage negotiation that nearly broke down at midnight, the contract I turned away because the ethics did not sit right — those decisions were made with two columns on the page. The first column was the numbers. The second column was a quieter question, which is whether I would be at peace with this decision when I knelt down beside the bed at the end of the day. The numbers can be argued about. The second column cannot.

A few verses have done particular service over the decades. *Whatever you do, work at it with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters* — that one, from Colossians, has been with me since the apprentice mornings at Sub Nigel, when my mother woke me before dawn to study and I did not always feel like opening the book. It is the same verse I quote, gently, to a young engineer who tells me he is just doing his job. There is no such thing as just doing your job. There is doing your job with all your heart, or there is doing it badly. The middle position does not exist.

*Trust in the Lord with all your heart, and lean not on your own understanding* has been the other steady companion, particularly in the seasons when the right next step was not visible. Every major transition in this career — the AECI leap from the mine, the Adcock decision after a casual Sunday-newspaper job application, the Kolosus headhunting call at forty-two, the move into Business Transformation contracts in the third act — has been taken at a moment when the path ahead was not obvious. I have learned, slowly, that the path becomes obvious only one step into it. You have to be willing to take the step before the visibility arrives. That is what faith, in working clothes, actually looks like.

And then there is the verse that has done the most work in the negotiation rooms — *do unto others as you would have them do unto you*. It is the oldest line in the book, and it remains the most useful management principle I have ever found. The shop steward across the table, the contractor whose schedule you are tightening, the manager you are letting go on Friday afternoon, the supplier whose price you are testing — all of them, without exception, deserve the same treatment you would want if the chair were swapped. I have signed industrial relations agreements I could have squeezed harder. I have left small margins on the table in supplier negotiations. I have, on at least three occasions in my career, written a personal reference for a person I had just retrenched. None of those decisions cost me a cent in the long arc. Every one of them came back, years later, in the form of a phone call, a reference, a contract, or a quiet recommendation in a room I was not in.

Faith, lived honestly, is not a marketing instrument. It is an operating system. It runs in the background of every decision, and the people around you can feel whether it is on or off, even if you never mention it.

## The leadership question of the AI age

I want to turn now to the question that, in 2026, is on the desk of every leader I speak to. The technology in front of us — large language models, autonomous agents, AI copilots embedded in every productivity tool — is moving faster than any new technology I have commissioned in a fifty-year working life. I have stood next to a German extrusion machine in 1980. I have signed off on a R200-million mogul line at Tiger Brands. I have lived through the introduction of ERP, of OEE, of FSSC, of Poka-Yoke as a daily discipline. I have seen change. This is not the same.

I have a phrase I have started using in the mentoring sessions I run with younger managers, and I will put it here so that you can quote it back to me when I am wrong. The leaders who use AI will replace the leaders who do not. That is not a prediction. That is already happening. A young manager who is fluent in a modern AI tool — who can interrogate a large data set, draft a board paper, summarise a hundred-page contract, build a financial model and stress-test it in an afternoon — has a leverage that the same manager, ten years ago, could not have dreamed of. The reverse is also true. A senior manager who refuses to learn the tool, who insists that this is something for the IT department or the next generation, is a senior manager who is choosing, by inaction, to be replaced.

I would like to be very clear here, because I do not want to be misread. I am not saying that AI replaces the human leader. It does not. I am saying that AI replaces the human leader who refuses to use it, in favour of one who does. The difference is everything.

I have, at seventy-three, made a deliberate decision to learn. I use the tools. I draft, I summarise, I research, I prepare. I run my reading list through them. I ask them to argue against me. I sometimes do not like the answer, which is when I know the tool is doing what it is supposed to do. The same engineer who once learned to read a vibration trace on a cyanide reactor in Modderfontein is now learning to read the output of a model that can summarise three decades of pharma manufacturing standards in eight seconds. The instrument has changed. The discipline of learning to use the instrument is the same.

But — and I want to put this in capital letters in your mind, because it is the one warning I want to leave with the next generation — the discipline of being a *leader* in the AI age is not the same as the discipline of being a *user*. The two are different qualifications.

Let me be specific. A model can draft your retrenchment letter. It cannot sit across from the person who is receiving it. A model can summarise a wage negotiation position. It cannot read the room at one a.m. when the shop steward needs to save face. A model can build you a perfect performance scorecard. It cannot recognise Sipho the fitter at the Friday morning team meeting in a way that makes him stand a little taller for the next six months. A model can write you a board paper that sounds correct. It cannot tell you whether you would be at peace with the decision when you kneel down beside the bed.

Empathy. Integrity. The human touch. The capacity to be present with another person in a difficult conversation. The capacity to look a worker in the eye and tell him that you trust him. The capacity to celebrate success down to the shop floor, to give a senior manager wings to fly, to hang a *Work Smart and Have Fun* banner above the office door in Port Elizabeth and mean every word of it. None of that is going to come out of a model in 2026, or 2036, or in my lifetime. It is going to come out of a leader who has, in the years before the meeting, done the unglamorous, repeated, daily work of becoming the kind of person another person can trust.

The Trust-and-Inspire philosophy I have laid out earlier in this book — over Command-and-Control, with the Nadella turnaround at Microsoft as the proof — is not weakened by the arrival of AI. It is sharpened by it. The technical leverage of the tool means that the only durable differentiator left for a senior leader is the quality of the relationship with the team. Everything that is technical can be automated. Almost nothing that is relational can be. If you are a young manager reading this, that is the most practical piece of career advice I can give you. Learn the tool until you are dangerous with it. Then put the tool down and learn to be present in a room. The combination of the two — fluency in the model, and presence with the people — is what the next generation of managing directors is going to look like.

## Key Focus Areas and Life Learnings — a final distillation

I have, over the years, kept a quiet list of what I would call my Key Focus Areas — the principles I would want a young manager to take with them out of a mentoring session. I have referred to most of them in passing in earlier chapters. Let me put them in one place, in plain language, for the reader who has come to the back of the book and wants the recipe on a single page.

- **Make the early decision and stay with it.** I decided, at sixteen, that the mine would be a place I started from and not a place I stayed in. That decision shaped fifty-seven years. Whatever your equivalent is, make it early and refuse to renegotiate it.
- **Under-promise and over-deliver, every time.** Thirty days early in Dusseldorf saved roughly R30 million in 1980s rand. The habit, repeated across a career, becomes a reputation. The reputation, repeated across a career, becomes a phone that keeps ringing.
- **Structure follows strategy.** Do not reorganise before you know what you are reorganising for. I have walked into businesses that had been through three restructurings in two years and still did not know what they were trying to be.
- **Hire for attitude, train for skill.** Qualifications tell you what a person has done. Attitude tells you what they will do. I have hired more people on the second than the first, and I have rarely regretted it.
- **Use Pareto. Always.** Twenty percent of the root causes will, almost without exception, account for eighty percent of the loss. Spend your weeks on the twenty.
- **Celebrate success down to the shop floor.** A small word of recognition to a fitter is worth more than a long memo from a director. Sipho will remember the handshake long after the bonus letter has been filed.
- **Industrial relations is not a side function — it is the function.** Every senior manager will, in the fullness of a career, learn this the hard way. Learn it the easy way, in your thirties, by sitting in on every negotiation you can.
- **Finish on a high note.** Walk out of every company with the standards higher than when you arrived, with the people better trained, and with a clean office. You may never set foot in the building again. Or, more often than you think, you may walk back in on a contract ten years later.
- **Relationships compound.** The colleague you treat well in your thirties is the colleague who will recruit you in your sixties. There is no other asset class in a career that compounds at that rate.
- **Lead with Trust-and-Inspire, not Command-and-Control.** People will give you their hands for a salary. They will give you their heads and their hearts only for a leader they trust.
- **Stay healthy.** The body and the mind are one balance sheet. You cannot run a complex factory if you cannot run yourself.
- **Read your bible. Or whatever your equivalent is.** Have a source of authority outside the spreadsheet, against which you measure the difficult decisions.

That, as plainly as I can put it, is the list. None of it is original. All of it has been tested.

## The unique-life-moments list

I want, briefly, to come back to a small, undeclared list I have kept in my head over the years — what I called, in the earlier sport chapter, my list of unique life moments. It is not a brag list. It is a list of moments that would have astonished the sixteen-year-old apprentice who was riding the cage down at Sub Nigel on his first morning underground.

The day Mr Mandela accepted a cheque for his seventy-fifth birthday celebrations in Port Elizabeth, with me standing in the small group of business leaders who had organised it, is on that list. To hand a cheque to the man who had walked out of Victor Verster eight years earlier and asked an entire country to choose forgiveness over the easier emotions — that is a moment a miner's son does not forget. The 1995 Rugby World Cup final at Ellis Park, where I sat with my management team as managing director and watched Francois Pienaar lift the Webb Ellis Cup from a President in a Springbok jersey, is on that list. The afternoon I stood in the centre of the field at Murrayfield in Scotland is on that list, for reasons that have less to do with rugby and more to do with how unlikely my life had looked from a mine house on the East Rand. The mornings I have spent at home with grandchildren on my lap are also on that list, undeclared, alongside the cheques and the cups.

When I look at the list now, I see one consistent thread. Every moment on it was made possible by a decision taken earlier, often much earlier, by a younger version of myself who had no idea that the moment was coming. That is the only secret I have ever found to a life of unique moments. You cannot plan them. You can only build, in the quiet years, the kind of life into which they eventually arrive.

## A word from the people I worked with

The testimonials that have come in over the years, from managers and shopfloor staff and shop stewards and chairmen, have humbled me more than any title ever did. I will not quote them at length here — they belong to the people who wrote them, and to the contracts under which we worked together. But I want to share, in summary, what they tend to have in common.

They almost never mention the financial outcome. They almost never mention the capex projects or the OEE improvements. What they mention is the way the work felt while it was being done. They mention being trusted. They mention being given wings to fly. They mention the *Work Smart and Have Fun* banner. They mention the senior manager who learned their name on day one, the engineer who stayed back to walk them through a procedure, the director who came to the shop floor on a Friday morning to hand-shake the team after a difficult week. They mention, in short, the things that no model can do.

That is the legacy I would most like to leave on the record. Not the cheques, not the cups, not the company logos. The way it felt to work with us.

## What comes next

I do not know what comes next. I say that with the same curiosity I felt as a sixteen-year-old apprentice at Sub Nigel, walking into the first morning of the trade and wondering, quietly and privately, just how far this might go. The clipboard has been many things since then — a German specification, a packing line drawing, a Paris leather negotiation, a chemical plant in Sasolburg, a peanut butter procurement spec, a porridge factory in Durban. The next clipboard may very well be an AI dashboard on a laptop, with a remote first conversation with a board I have not yet met. That is fine with me. The work is the same.

What I do know is what I would like the closing principle of this book to be, for the young reader and the older reader alike. Anything is possible. That is the opening line of the foreword, and it is the closing line of the epilogue, and it is the only sentence I would want underlined on the inside cover of every copy that leaves the printer.

Anything is possible for the person who decides early, works consistently, builds relationships, stays healthy, stays faithful, learns the new instrument when it arrives, and refuses to let the ladder they are currently on tell them what ladder they are allowed to climb to. That sentence is not a piece of motivational rhetoric. It is a working description of a fifty-seven-year career, written by a man who started in a mine house on the East Rand and is, at seventy-three, still being asked to lead.

The Lord has given me a faithful wife, four children, nine grandchildren, three great-grandchildren, fifty-one years of marriage, a body of experience that, by His grace, still has something to offer, and the privilege of having spent a working life in the company of decent, hard-working South Africans.

The cake has long since been eaten, the balloons have long since come down, and the phone, for what it is worth, is still ringing.

I am going to keep answering it.

And to the apprentice Boilermaker reading this in his overalls on a Sunday afternoon, with his trade test ahead of him and the rest of his life ahead of him after that, I would like to say one last thing, in the plainest English I can muster.

The ladder is yours. Climb it.

characters.name: Phil de Jager
characters.description: South African business leader, born around 1952, the author and narrator; rose from sixteen-year-old apprentice Boilermaker at Sub Nigel Gold Mine to Managing Director and contract Business Transformation Specialist still active at seventy-three.
characters.personality: Disciplined, curious, competitive, faith-driven, relentlessly consistent, and warmly people-oriented; instinctively analytical with a bias to action and a refusal to be intimidated by background or scale.
characters.background: Youngest of five children of a miner father and stay-at-home mother on the East Rand; Mechanical Engineering Diploma, diploma in Company Direction, all final-year subjects of an International MBA and an intensive MBA short course at the University of Cape Town.
characters.goals: To prove that humble origins are no barrier to a fulfilling life, to leave every company stronger than he found it through Trust-and-Inspire leadership, and to use his late-career contract work and a new LinkedIn-fronted Business Transformation Company to mentor the next generation.
characters.conflicts: Reconciling the time demanded by ambition, overseas travel and three sports with the time owed to his wife and children; resisting friend-group peer pressure during his studies; navigating loss-making companies, retrenchments and a culture clash with Continental Oils that closed one chapter cleanly.

characters.name: Phil's Mother
characters.description: A shy, industrious stay-at-home mother who woke Phil before dawn to study and held the household together on a miner's wage.
characters.personality: Introverted, devout, fiercely industrious, and quietly relentless about her youngest son's education.
characters.background: Raised five children in semi-detached mine housing on the East Rand; died a year after her husband at fifty-four.
characters.goals: To give her children, especially her youngest, a normal upbringing and a path off the mine through study.
characters.conflicts: Could not survive long after her husband's death; her early loss arrived in the middle of Phil's transition from AECI to Adcock Ingram.

characters.name: Phil's Father (the Miner)
characters.description: A devoted Christian miner at Sub Nigel Gold Mine who spent most of his working life underground and died of lung disease at sixty-three.
characters.personality: Outgoing relative to his wife, simple, trustworthy, devout, and quietly proud; a man of the church and the shaft rather than of words.
characters.background: Long-serving South African gold miner; father of five; provided a low-income but morally ordered home that shaped Phil's work ethic and faith.
characters.goals: To raise his children inside the church and to see at least some of them rise beyond the underground life he knew.
characters.conflicts: The lung disease that ultimately killed him became a direct reason Phil left mining for AECI; his early death from underground exposure cast a long shadow over Phil's career choices.

characters.name: Antonette de Jager
characters.description: Phil's wife of fifty-one years, the steady centre of the family through every move, contract and overseas trip.
characters.personality: Prayerful, disciplined, unselfish and deeply faith-driven; quietly insistent on the family's relationship with God as non-negotiable.
characters.background: Married Phil when he was twenty-two; raised four children largely on her own while Phil travelled internationally and worked long hours at AECI, Adcock Ingram, Kolosus and Tiger Brands.
characters.goals: To raise a Christ-centred family, to support Phil's career without sacrificing the children's stability, and to pass faith and discipline to the next generations.
characters.conflicts: Carrying the domestic load through Phil's frequent international travel and three-sport sporting career, and managing the family's relocation traumas, especially the Port Elizabeth move that left two grown children behind in Johannesburg.
